labels.rec_internal_links_missing_in_ssr: Internal links missing from raw HTML (JS-only)
labels.rec_semantic_structure_missing_in_ssr: Semantic tags missing from raw HTML

descriptions.noindex_added: A noindex directive was added (robots/googlebot meta tag or X-Robots-Tag HTTP header). The page will disappear from Google results within days.
descriptions.status_code_changed: The HTTP status code changed (e.g. 200 to 404 or 301). Critical for server errors (4xx/5xx), warning for redirects (3xx).
descriptions.canonical_missing: The canonical tag is gone. Duplicate content risk — Google no longer knows which URL to index.
descriptions.canonical_changed: The canonical tag points to a different URL. May be intentional (migration) or accidental.
descriptions.meta_title_missing: The meta title is gone. Google will display an auto-generated title in search results.
descriptions.soft_404: The page returns a 200 status but displays a "page not found" message. Google treats it as deleted.
descriptions.redirect_to_homepage: The page redirects to the homepage. All content and rankings for this URL are lost.
descriptions.page_redirected: A page still declared in your sitemap has started redirecting to another URL: the sitemap presents an address that bounces as canonical. Intentional removal — take it out of the sitemap; otherwise, often a broken route. A page that is redirected AND removed from the sitemap is a clean removal: no alert.
descriptions.js_redirect_detected: The page responds 200 but its JavaScript redirects the browser elsewhere. Google only follows it after rendering (delayed, or never if rendering fails) and AI systems read the original HTML, not the destination: machines and humans see two different pages.
descriptions.redirect_broken: A cleanly removed page (redirect + removed from the sitemap) now returns a 4xx error: its redirect broke. Link equity from backlinks pointing to the old URL is no longer passed — typically a refactor that deleted the redirect rule or its target.
descriptions.rec_redirect_temporary: A page removed from the sitemap redirects with a 302/307 (temporary). Google keeps the old URL indexed and is slower to consolidate signals toward the target. If the removal is permanent, a 301 (permanent) redirect passes link equity cleanly.
descriptions.rec_unclean_removal: A page removed from the sitemap returns a bare 404. It works, but the signal is ambiguous: Google keeps re-crawling it for weeks. Permanent deletion — return 410 (faster deindexing); moved content — 301 (preserves backlink equity).
descriptions.rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict: This page is declared in your sitemap but carries a noindex directive (meta tag or X-Robots-Tag header): you are asking Google to index it and ignore it at the same time. Wasted crawl budget and polluted GSC indexing reports. Remove it from the sitemap, or remove the noindex.
descriptions.ssr_rendering_failed: The HTML returned by the server (SSR) is almost empty even though the page works in the browser. Google indexes an empty page.
descriptions.ssr_content_mismatch: The SSR content represents less than 10% of the content displayed by the browser. Google sees a heavily stripped-down version of the page.
descriptions.h1_missing: The H1 is gone. It is the main title of the page — a strong SEO signal for Google.
descriptions.h1_multiple: Multiple H1 tags detected. This dilutes the SEO signal — a page should have only one main title.
descriptions.h1_changed: The H1 text changed. May be intentional (editorial update) or accidental (template bug).
descriptions.viewport_missing: The viewport tag is gone. The page is no longer mobile-friendly — Google penalizes this under mobile-first indexing.
descriptions.thin_content: The content dropped below 200 words. Pages with little content are less likely to rank.
descriptions.content_removed: More than 50% of the content is gone. Massive deletion — probably a deploy or template bug.
descriptions.hreflang_removed: All hreflang tags were removed. International targeting is broken — Google may serve the wrong language.
descriptions.hreflang_changed: The languages declared in the hreflang tags changed. May be intentional (language added/removed) or accidental.
descriptions.https_mixed_content: Images, scripts or styles are loaded over HTTP on an HTTPS page. The security padlock disappears and browsers may block these resources.
descriptions.meta_description_missing: The meta description is gone. Google generates an automatic snippet instead: possible impact on click-through rate, but no deindexing and no ranking loss.
descriptions.structured_data_removed: The structured data (JSON-LD) was removed. Loss of rich results (stars, prices, FAQ) in Google results.
descriptions.structured_data_error: The JSON-LD is invalid (syntax error). Google ignores malformed structured data.
descriptions.og_image_removed: The Open Graph image was removed. Social media shares will no longer display a visual.
descriptions.og_title_removed: The Open Graph title was removed. Social platforms will fall back to the meta title.
descriptions.meta_refresh_detected: A meta refresh tag redirects the page. Bad practice — use an HTTP 301 redirect instead.
descriptions.robots_txt_changed: The robots directives changed (other than noindex). Check that the right crawlers still have access to the right pages.
descriptions.heading_hierarchy_broken: Skipped heading level (e.g. H1 to H3 without an H2). Micro-impact on accessibility; near-zero effect on ranking.
descriptions.lang_attribute_missing: The HTML lang attribute is gone. Google may misidentify the page language.
descriptions.lang_attribute_changed: The lang attribute changed (e.g. fr to en). Can break language targeting if accidental.
descriptions.word_count_changed: The word count changed by 30 to 50%. Notable change but not catastrophic — worth checking.
descriptions.charset_missing: The charset declaration is gone. Risk of garbled characters (accents, emojis) in some browsers.
descriptions.ssr_title_mismatch: The title in the raw HTML (sent by the server) differs from the title displayed after JavaScript loads. Google indexes the raw HTML version.
descriptions.ssr_meta_description_mismatch: The meta description in the raw HTML differs from the one rendered after JavaScript loads. Google uses the raw HTML version.
descriptions.meta_title_changed: The meta title was modified. May be intentional (A/B test, rebrand) or accidental (template bug).
descriptions.meta_description_changed: The meta description was modified. Rarely a problem — usually an editorial update.
descriptions.ssr_blocked: The crawler received an anti-bot challenge page (Cloudflare, Akamai, etc.) instead of the real content. Data for this page is not reliable.
descriptions.llms_txt_removed: The /llms.txt file was deleted. Optional file: no major LLM confirms consuming it to date (a proposal that has not been adopted) — mainly useful for documentation and agents, not a citation factor.
descriptions.ai_crawlers_blocked_changed: The robots.txt now blocks additional AI citation crawlers (live search/browse from OpenAI, Perplexity, Anthropic) — reduces your visibility in ChatGPT search, Perplexity and Claude. Training crawlers (GPTBot, Google-Extended...) are not monitored: blocking them does not affect citation.
descriptions.robots_blocks_googlebot: The robots.txt has started blocking Googlebot (Disallow under User-agent: Googlebot or *) on a previously allowed path. Risk of an entire section being deindexed.
descriptions.faq_schema_removed: The FAQPage schema was removed. Google no longer shows FAQ rich results in the SERP: direct impact is low, but the removal may signal a component deleted by mistake. The visible Q&A content remains readable by Google and LLMs.
descriptions.structured_data_author_removed: The author disappeared from the structured data. AI systems cite anonymous content less — a credibility loss.
descriptions.rec_img_alt_audit: Some images have no alt attribute. Important for accessibility and image SEO. Warning if 10+ images are affected.
descriptions.rec_title_length_audit: The meta title is too short (< 15 characters) or too long (> 60 characters). Google may truncate or rewrite it.
descriptions.rec_description_length_audit: The meta description is too short (< 50 characters) or too long (> 160 characters). Ideally between 120 and 155 characters.
descriptions.rec_h1_missing_audit: No H1 on the page (checked on the rendered DOM after JavaScript hydration). The main title is a strong SEO signal — every page should have one.
descriptions.rec_h1_missing_in_ssr: The H1 is missing or empty in the raw HTML sent by your server but filled in after JavaScript loads. Google may see it with a delay (24 hours to several weeks). LLMs (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude) probably never see it — they mostly read the raw HTML.
descriptions.rec_content_missing_in_ssr: Most of the body text only exists after JavaScript runs (absent from the raw HTML). AI crawlers (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude) read the raw HTML and will probably never see this content.
descriptions.rec_title_missing_in_ssr: The <title> is missing from the raw HTML sent by your server but filled in after JavaScript loads. Google may see it with a delay and LLMs (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude) probably never see it — they mostly read the raw HTML. Fix: render the title server-side (SSR).
descriptions.rec_description_missing_in_ssr: The meta description is missing from the raw HTML but present after JavaScript loads. Google and LLMs mostly read the raw HTML — your search snippet and AI visibility may suffer. Fix: render the meta description server-side (SSR).
descriptions.rec_internal_links_missing_in_ssr: Your internal links are not in the raw HTML sent by your server — they only appear after JavaScript loads. Google discovers your pages more slowly. Fix: render menu, footer and navigation links server-side (SSR).
descriptions.rec_structured_data_missing_in_ssr: Structured data (JSON-LD) injected by JavaScript only. AI systems (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude) probably never see it — they mostly read the raw HTML. For better AI visibility (GEO), render the JSON-LD server-side.
descriptions.rec_img_alt_missing_in_ssr: Images added by JavaScript have no alt attribute. Google Image Search will probably not index them (it reads the raw HTML), and visually impaired users cannot understand them. Fix: add an alt attribute and render the images server-side if possible.
descriptions.rec_semantic_structure_missing_in_ssr: Semantic tags (main, header, footer) missing from the raw HTML sent by your server — they only appear after JavaScript loads. Google and screen readers (accessibility) understand your page structure better when these tags are present from the first render. Fix: structure your layout with these tags server-side.
descriptions.rec_favicon_missing_audit: No favicon detected. Minor impact, but visible in browser tabs and Google results.
descriptions.rec_semantic_structure_audit: Missing semantic tags (main, header, footer). Improves accessibility and helps search engines understand the structure.
descriptions.rec_structured_data_missing_audit: No structured data (JSON-LD) detected. You are missing out on rich results in Google.
descriptions.rec_og_missing_audit: Missing Open Graph tags (og:title, og:description or og:image). Social shares will be less attractive.
descriptions.rec_internal_links_audit: Fewer than 3 internal links on the page. Warning if no links at all — the page is orphaned and poorly linked.
descriptions.rec_llms_txt_missing: No /llms.txt file detected. Speculative convention: its use by major LLMs is not confirmed to date (a proposal not adopted by Google/OpenAI). Mainly useful for documentation and agents.
descriptions.rec_ai_crawlers_blocked: The robots.txt blocks AI citation crawlers (OAI-SearchBot, ChatGPT-User, PerplexityBot, Perplexity-User, Claude-SearchBot, Claude-User) — reduces your visibility in ChatGPT search, Perplexity and Claude answers. Training crawlers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, Google-Extended...) are not monitored: blocking them does not affect citation.
descriptions.rec_structured_data_incomplete: Structured data exists but is incomplete (missing author, date or publisher). AI systems use these fields to cite your content.
descriptions.rec_faq_schema_missing: Content page (more than 300 words) without a FAQPage schema. If the content addresses recurring questions, structuring it as clear Q&A helps AI answer engines pick it up. The FAQPage schema no longer triggers rich results in the SERP: the editorial structure is what matters.
descriptions.rec_citation_signals_missing: Several citation signals are missing (author, date, sources). AI systems favor attributable, dated content.
descriptions.rec_content_structure_audit: The content has neither subheadings (H2) nor lists. AI systems extract information better when content is well structured.
descriptions.perf_page_weight_explosion: The page weight increased sharply. A heavy page loads slowly, especially on mobile and 4G.
descriptions.rec_perf_page_heavy: The page exceeds 1.6 MB (Lighthouse threshold), excessive beyond 5 MB. Compressing images and trimming JavaScript would speed up loading.

noindex_added.constat: A noindex directive appeared on this page (robots meta tag or X-Robots-Tag header).
noindex_added.pourquoi: Google removes the page from its index within days: all its organic traffic disappears. If the directive is accidental (staging config deployed, plugin), the loss goes unnoticed until traffic drops.
noindex_added.action: Check whether the noindex is intentional; if not, remove it and redeploy immediately.
noindex_added.gain: The page stays in the Google index and keeps its existing traffic.
noindex_added.seoTitle: Noindex: Real Effect on Google & Accidental Blocking
noindex_added.h1: Noindex added: your page will drop from Google within days
noindex_added.metaDescription: A noindex directive removes the page from Google on the next crawl. Real effect, causes of an accidental noindex, and how to fix it.
noindex_added.tldr: A noindex directive removes the page from Google’s results as soon as Googlebot recrawls it, often within days. Only use it when deindexing is intended; otherwise remove it and redeploy immediately.
whenNotAProblem: The page should NOT be indexed (cart, internal search results, thank-you page, technical duplicate).
The noindex is temporary and intentional (page in preparation) and you know it will be removed.
You just added the page and are managing its indexing in stages.

exemple.avant:
<!-- The page leaves the index -->
<meta name="robots" content="noindex, follow">
exemple.apres:
<!-- The page stays indexable -->
<meta name="robots" content="index, follow">
exemple.note: A noindex can also come from an X-Robots-Tag HTTP header, invisible in the HTML: check both.

actionSteps: Confirm whether the noindex is intended for this specific URL.
If it’s accidental, find its source: robots meta in the template, server X-Robots-Tag header, or a staging config deployed to production.
Remove the directive and redeploy.
Request a fresh crawl in Search Console to speed up reindexing.
noindex_added.scanHook: Does an important page carry a noindex by mistake? Enter a URL: Seogard reads the robots meta AND the X-Robots-Tag header and tells you in 30 seconds whether Google is allowed to index. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: How long before a noindexed page disappears?
faq.a: Usually within days, once Googlebot recrawls the page and picks up the directive. Frequently crawled pages drop faster.

faq.q: Should I also block the page in robots.txt?
faq.a: No, it’s counterproductive: if robots.txt blocks crawling, Google can’t read the noindex and may keep the page indexed. Keep the page crawlable with its noindex.

status_code_changed.constat: The page's HTTP status code changed (e.g. 200 to 404, 500 or a redirect).
status_code_changed.pourquoi: A repeated 4xx/5xx error gets the page dropped from the index and cuts its traffic. LLMs that re-visit the page to cite it get an error and exclude it from their answers.
status_code_changed.action: Identify the cause of the new status code (deploy, deleted route, server) and restore the 200 — or set up a 301 redirect to the equivalent page.
status_code_changed.gain: Google and AI systems keep accessing the content; the URL's accumulated authority is preserved.
status_code_changed.seoTitle: HTTP Status Changed: 200 Now 404 or 301 (What to Do)
status_code_changed.h1: HTTP status changed: when a page turns into a 404, 500 or redirect
status_code_changed.metaDescription: A page's HTTP status changed: a 200 became a 404, 500 or 3xx. Google may deindex it and cut its traffic. Causes, severity and how to fix it.
status_code_changed.tldr: The HTTP status returned by the page changed: a 4xx/5xx error drops it from the index and cuts its traffic, a 3xx makes it redirect. Identify the cause (deploy, deleted route, server) and restore the 200, or set up a clean 301 redirect.
whenNotAProblem: The 301 redirect is intentional, points to an equivalent page, and the URL was removed from the sitemap (clean removal).
You returned a deliberate 410 to signal a permanent, intended deletion.
You are on the first crawl of the page: Seogard has no baseline to compare against yet.

exemple.avant:
GET /category/shoes HTTP/1.1
-> HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error
exemple.apres:
GET /category/shoes HTTP/1.1
-> HTTP/1.1 200 OK
exemple.note: A repeated 500 or 404 drops the page from the index; the goal is to restore a 200, or a clean 301 if the URL should disappear.

actionSteps: Reproduce the request on the affected URL (curl -I or the Network tab) to confirm the status the server returns.
Identify the cause: recent deploy, deleted route, database, or application error.
Restore a 200 if the page should live, or set a 301 redirect to the closest equivalent if it should go away.
Re-crawl the page to confirm the status is back to normal.
status_code_changed.scanHook: Is one of your pages suddenly returning an error? Enter a URL: Seogard checks the real HTTP status of every page and alerts you the moment a 200 flips to a 404, 500 or redirect. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does a 404 make a page lose its rankings?
faq.a: Yes if the error persists: Google eventually deindexes the URL and its traffic disappears. A quick fix or a 301 redirect preserves the accumulated authority.

faq.q: What is the SEO difference between a 4xx and a 5xx error?
faq.a: A 4xx (like 404) says the page does not exist; a 5xx says the server crashed. Both are serious, but a repeated 5xx can also slow the crawl of your whole site.

canonical_missing.constat: The canonical tag on this page is gone.
canonical_missing.pourquoi: Without a canonical, Google picks the URL to index on its own among the variants (parameters, www, trailing slash). On a site with tracking or facets, the signal gets diluted across duplicates and rankings become unstable.
canonical_missing.action: Restore the self-referencing canonical tag in the affected template.
canonical_missing.gain: Google concentrates all the signal on a single URL — stable, predictable rankings.
canonical_missing.seoTitle: Missing Canonical Tag: Duplicate Risk & How to Fix
canonical_missing.h1: Missing canonical: Google no longer knows which URL to index
canonical_missing.metaDescription: Without a canonical tag, Google picks which URL variant to index on its own, diluting the signal. Why it’s a risk and how to fix it.
canonical_missing.tldr: Without a canonical tag, Google picks which of your URL variants to index on its own (parameters, www, trailing slash), which can dilute or duplicate the signal. Restore a self-referencing canonical to point to the reference URL.
whenNotAProblem: The page has no URL variants (no tracking parameters, facets, or www/non-www duplication).
A clean 301 redirect already consolidates all variants to a single URL.
The page is intentionally not indexed: canonical isn’t the concern then.

exemple.avant:
<!-- No canonical: Google guesses -->
<head>
  <title>Trail running shoes</title>
</head>
exemple.apres:
<!-- Self-referencing canonical -->
<link rel="canonical" href="https://example.com/trail-shoes">
exemple.note: The canonical must point to the clean URL (without tracking parameters) you want indexed.

actionSteps: Identify the affected template (product, article, category) and the reference URL to index.
Add a self-referencing <link rel="canonical"> in the <head>, rendered server-side.
Make sure it points to the clean absolute URL, not a variant with parameters.
Re-crawl to confirm the canonical is present and consistent.
canonical_missing.scanHook: Do all your pages have their canonical? Enter a URL: Seogard checks the canonical tag in the raw HTML and the render and flags inconsistencies in 30 seconds. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Should a page have a canonical pointing to itself?
faq.a: Yes, the best practice is a self-referencing canonical on every indexable page: it removes any ambiguity about the reference URL.

faq.q: Does a missing canonical cost rankings?
faq.a: Not directly, but it lets Google split the signal across duplicate URLs, which can make rankings unstable.

canonical_changed.constat: The canonical points to a different URL than the one observed on the previous crawl.
canonical_changed.pourquoi: If the change is intentional (migration, consolidation), all is well. If accidental, you are telling Google to index ANOTHER page instead of this one: the current page can be deindexed in favor of the target.
canonical_changed.action: Confirm that the new canonical target is intentional; otherwise restore the self-reference.
canonical_changed.gain: No silent deindexing: every indexable page keeps its signal.
canonical_changed.seoTitle: Canonical Tag Changed: Intentional or Deindexing?
canonical_changed.h1: Canonical changed: the page now points to another URL
canonical_changed.metaDescription: A page's canonical tag now points to a different URL. Fine during a migration, but if accidental it is silent deindexing. How to check and fix it.
canonical_changed.tldr: The page's canonical tag now names a different URL: if it is intentional (migration, consolidation) all is well, otherwise you are telling Google to index another page instead and the current one can drop from the index. Confirm the new target is intended, otherwise restore the self-reference.
whenNotAProblem: Intentional migration or consolidation: the new canonical target is the page you want indexed.
A paginated page or variant (filter, sort) that should legitimately point to its main version.
First crawl: with no previous baseline, Seogard does not flag a change.

exemple.avant:
<!-- on page /product-a -->
<link rel="canonical" href="https://site.com/product-b" />
exemple.apres:
<!-- on page /product-a -->
<link rel="canonical" href="https://site.com/product-a" />
exemple.note: If /product-a should be indexed, its canonical must reference itself; pointing to /product-b gets it deindexed in favor of the other page.

actionSteps: Open the page source and find the <link rel="canonical"> tag and its new target.
Decide whether that target is intended: a planned migration or consolidation, or an accidental change.
If accidental, restore the self-reference (canonical = the page's own URL) in the SEO template.
Re-crawl to confirm the canonical points to the right URL.
canonical_changed.scanHook: Do your canonicals point where you think? Enter a URL: Seogard reads the canonical tag on every page and alerts you the moment it changes target, before Google deindexes the wrong one. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does a canonical to another page really deindex the page?
faq.a: Yes: the canonical is a strong instruction to Google, which consolidates signals toward the target and often removes the original page from the index.

faq.q: How do I know if the canonical change was intentional?
faq.a: Check whether it lines up with a planned migration or redesign. If nobody decided to redirect indexing to another URL, it is accidental.

meta_title_missing.constat: The page's meta title is gone.
meta_title_missing.pourquoi: The title is the most direct relevance signal for Google and the clickable text in search results. Without it, Google generates an approximate title — often less relevant — and CTR drops. LLMs also rely on the title to understand and cite the page.
meta_title_missing.action: Restore the <title> tag in the template (check the framework's SEO/head component).
meta_title_missing.gain: You regain control of the title displayed in Google and read by AI systems.
meta_title_missing.seoTitle: Meta Title Removed: Google Rewrites Your Title (Fix)
meta_title_missing.h1: Meta title removed: Google generates a title for you
meta_title_missing.metaDescription: A page's meta title is gone. Google then shows an auto-generated title, often less relevant, and click-through drops. How to restore the tag.
meta_title_missing.tldr: The page's meta title is gone: Google generates an approximate title in its place, often less relevant, and click-through in search results suffers. Restore the <title> tag in the template to regain control of the title shown to users and read by AI systems.
whenNotAProblem: A purely app or back-office page that is not meant to be indexed.
A page blocked from indexing (noindex) where the title has no effect in results anyway.
First crawl: with no baseline, Seogard does not alert on a missing title.

exemple.avant:
<head>
  <!-- no title tag -->
</head>
exemple.apres:
<head>
  <title>Men's trail running shoes - MyStore</title>
</head>
exemple.note: Without a <title>, Google builds one from the content or links; it rarely reflects your key message.

actionSteps: View the page source and confirm there is no <title> tag in the <head>.
Find why it disappeared: the framework's SEO/head component, an empty variable, a template condition.
Restore a unique, descriptive <title> (main keyword then brand) and redeploy.
Re-crawl to confirm the title is present again.
meta_title_missing.scanHook: Do all your pages have a title? Enter a URL: Seogard checks the <title> in the raw HTML of every page and alerts you the moment it disappears, before Google rewrites it. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does Google make up a title when mine is missing?
faq.a: Yes: it generates one from the H1, the content or the anchor text of links pointing to the page, which is often less relevant than yours.

faq.q: Does the title really affect rankings?
faq.a: It is one of the most direct relevance signals for Google and the clickable text in results; LLMs also rely on it to understand and cite the page.

soft_404.constat: The page returns a 200 status but displays a "page not found" message.
soft_404.pourquoi: Google detects these "soft 404s" and treats them as deleted pages: deindexing, while your HTTP monitoring tools see nothing (the status is 200). It is the classic blind spot of SPAs.
soft_404.action: Return a real 404/410 status for content that no longer exists, or restore the content if the page should exist.
soft_404.gain: The Google index reflects your actual site; crawl budget is no longer wasted on dead pages.
soft_404.seoTitle: Soft 404: Meaning, Causes & How to Fix It
soft_404.h1: Soft 404: a "not found" page Google treats as gone
soft_404.metaDescription: A soft 404 returns 200 while showing "page not found": Google deindexes it. Meaning, common causes and how to fix it.
soft_404.tldr: A soft 404 is a page that returns 200 while showing "not found"-style content: Google treats it as removed and drops it from the index. Return a real 404/410, or restore the content if the page should exist.
whenNotAProblem: Seogard flagged the page but it genuinely has useful, unique content: it’s a false positive, no action needed.
It’s a deliberately lightweight but legitimate page with real content of its own.

exemple.avant:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK

Page not found

This product no longer exists.
exemple.apres:
HTTP/1.1 410 Gone

Product removed

This product is no longer available.
exemple.note: What matters is the HTTP status: "not found" content must come with a 404 or 410, not a 200.

actionSteps: Find the URLs flagged as "soft 404" in the Search Console indexing report.
For content that is genuinely gone, return a 404 (not found) or 410 (permanently removed).
For a page that should exist, restore its real content.
On an SPA, make sure the server returns the right HTTP status, not just a JavaScript display.
soft_404.scanHook: Do some of your pages return 200 while being empty? Enter a URL: Seogard catches soft 404s that your HTTP monitoring misses. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Why does Google call it a soft 404 if my page returns 200?
faq.a: Because the content looks like an error page ("not found", empty): Google trusts the content, not just the status, and treats it as removed.

faq.q: Is a 404 or a 410 better?
faq.a: A 404 means "not found" (maybe temporary); a 410 means "permanently gone" and speeds up deindexing. Use 410 when the removal is permanent.

redirect_to_homepage.constat: The page redirects to the homepage.
redirect_to_homepage.pourquoi: Google treats a blanket redirect to the homepage as a soft 404: the original URL's content, rankings and backlinks are lost. It is a frequent symptom of a broken route after a redesign.
redirect_to_homepage.action: Restore the page, or 301-redirect to the closest equivalent page (never the homepage).
redirect_to_homepage.gain: The URL's authority is passed to relevant content instead of being lost.
redirect_to_homepage.seoTitle: Redirect to Homepage: a Soft 404 That Kills the URL
redirect_to_homepage.h1: Redirect to homepage: the URL and its SEO go up in smoke
redirect_to_homepage.metaDescription: A page redirects to the homepage. Google treats it as a soft 404: the URL's content, rankings and backlinks are lost. Cause and how to fix it.
redirect_to_homepage.tldr: The page redirects to the homepage: Google treats this blanket redirect as a soft 404, and the original URL's content, rankings and backlinks are lost. Restore the page, or 301-redirect to the closest equivalent page, never to the home.
whenNotAProblem: The home genuinely is the closest equivalent (e.g. a subpage merged into the homepage) — a rare case.
An accepted temporary redirect during short maintenance, on a non-strategic URL.
The URL is a disposable marketing link (finished campaign) you knowingly send to the home, with no content or backlinks to preserve.

exemple.avant:
GET /shoe-size-guide
-> HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: https://site.com/
exemple.apres:
GET /shoe-size-guide
-> HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: https://site.com/help/size-guide
exemple.note: Redirecting to the home wipes the content; redirecting to the closest page passes authority to relevant content.

actionSteps: Check the redirect destination (curl -I or the Network tab): does it confirm a jump to the site root?
Decide the page's fate: restore it if still useful, or choose a relevant equivalent target.
Replace the redirect to the home with a 301 to the closest page by topic.
Re-crawl to confirm the page no longer redirects to the homepage.
redirect_to_homepage.scanHook: Are some of your pages landing on the home? Enter a URL: Seogard follows each page's redirects and alerts you when one points to the homepage, a classic sign of a broken route. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Why is a redirect to the home treated as a soft 404?
faq.a: Because the home does not answer the original URL's intent: Google decides the expected content no longer exists and denies the benefit of a real redirect.

faq.q: Is a 404 better than a redirect to the home?
faq.a: Often yes: a 404 is honest and lets Google clean up properly, whereas a redirect to the home wastes crawl budget and blurs the signals.

page_redirected.constat: A page still declared in your sitemap has started redirecting to another URL.
page_redirected.pourquoi: Your sitemap tells Google "this URL is canonical, crawl it" while it bounces elsewhere: contradictory signals, crawl budget wasted on an address that no longer really exists. Two possible readings: either the redirect is intentional (migration, content merge) and the sitemap is lagging behind; or it is not, and it is often a broken route, a page deleted by mistake or a redesign that moved the URL without anyone noticing. A page that is 301-redirected AND removed from the sitemap, however, is a clean removal: Seogard does not flag it.
page_redirected.action: If the redirect is intentional: remove the URL from the sitemap (and check that the redirect is a 301 to the equivalent page). Otherwise: restore the original page.
page_redirected.gain: The sitemap and the server tell the same story: Google focuses its crawl on real pages, and an accidental redirect no longer goes unnoticed.
page_redirected.seoTitle: Page Redirected but Still in the Sitemap (What to Do)
page_redirected.h1: Page redirected: the sitemap declares a URL that bounces
page_redirected.metaDescription: A page still listed in your sitemap started redirecting elsewhere. Contradictory signals for Google: if intended, remove it from the sitemap; otherwise, broken route.
page_redirected.tldr: A page still declared in your sitemap started redirecting to another URL: the sitemap presents a bouncing address as canonical, which wastes crawl budget. If the redirect is intended, remove the URL from the sitemap; otherwise it is often a broken route to restore.
whenNotAProblem: The redirect is intended and the URL has already been removed from the sitemap: that is a clean removal, and Seogard does not alert on it.
A migration in progress where the sitemap will soon be regenerated with the new URLs.
A 301 redirect to the equivalent page, pending the already scheduled sitemap update.

exemple.avant:
<!-- sitemap.xml -->
<url><loc>https://site.com/product-a</loc></url>
<!-- but /product-a returns 301 to /product-b -->
exemple.apres:
<!-- sitemap.xml -->
<url><loc>https://site.com/product-b</loc></url>
<!-- /product-a removed; 301 kept -->
exemple.note: The sitemap should list only URLs that answer with a 200; the old one can keep redirecting, outside the sitemap.

actionSteps: Confirm the redirect on the listed URL (curl -I) and note its destination.
Decide: intended redirect (migration) or broken route to fix?
If intended, remove the URL from the sitemap and check the redirect is a 301 to the equivalent page; otherwise, restore the original page.
Regenerate the sitemap and re-crawl to clear the inconsistency.
page_redirected.scanHook: Does your sitemap list URLs that redirect? Enter a URL: Seogard compares your sitemap pages with the real status they return and spots the ones that bounce instead of answering. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Why should a redirecting URL not stay in the sitemap?
faq.a: The sitemap is a list of canonical URLs to index; leaving a redirecting address in it sends contradictory signals and wastes Google's crawl budget.

faq.q: Is a redirect in the sitemap serious?
faq.a: Not catastrophic on its own, but it is a frequent symptom of a broken route or a lagging sitemap; worth watching so pages do not disappear unnoticed.

js_redirect_detected.constat: The page responds 200 with content, but its JavaScript redirects the browser to another URL.
js_redirect_detected.pourquoi: The server signals no redirect (no 3xx): to the HTTP fetch and to AI systems, this page is normal content — which they read and may index or cite — while humans get sent elsewhere. Google can follow a JavaScript redirect, but only after rendering the page, in a delayed second wave: it may index the wrong page in the meantime, and never sees the redirect if rendering fails. AI systems, which read the raw HTML, do not follow it at all. A JavaScript redirect is therefore an ambiguous, fragile signal, often inherited from an SPA router or a forgotten `window.location`.
js_redirect_detected.action: Replace the JavaScript redirect with a real 301/302 server-side redirect, or render the final content directly on the requested URL.
js_redirect_detected.gain: Machines and humans see the same page: no more indexing of the wrong URL, no more AI systems reading content that does not match the destination.
js_redirect_detected.seoTitle: JavaScript Redirect: Machines and Humans See 2 Pages
js_redirect_detected.h1: JavaScript redirect: the server says 200, the JS sends elsewhere
js_redirect_detected.metaDescription: A page responds 200 but its JavaScript redirects the browser elsewhere. Google follows it late or never, AI reads the original HTML. Why it is fragile.
js_redirect_detected.tldr: The page responds 200 with content but its JavaScript redirects the browser elsewhere: Google only follows it after rendering (delayed, or never) and AI systems read the original HTML, not the destination. Replace it with a real 301/302 server-side redirect, or render the final content directly.
whenNotAProblem: A redirect triggered by a user action (form, login) on a page not meant for indexing.
A purely internal app page (dashboard, account area) outside the indexing scope.
The target is the localized version of the same page (language redirect), not a content change.

exemple.avant:
<!-- /promo responds 200 but -->
<script>window.location.href = '/shop'</script>
exemple.apres:
GET /promo
-> HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: https://site.com/shop
exemple.note: A real server-side redirect is seen immediately by Google and AI systems; the JavaScript window.location is seen late, or never.

actionSteps: Compare the raw HTML (200) with the browser render: does the page load content before a script sends it elsewhere?
Locate the JavaScript redirect (SPA router, window.location, delayed meta refresh).
Replace it with a server-side 301 (permanent) or 302 (temporary) redirect, or serve the final content directly on the requested URL.
Re-crawl to confirm the server returns the right status, with no leftover JavaScript redirect.
js_redirect_detected.scanHook: Does one of your pages redirect in JavaScript without telling the server? Enter a URL: Seogard compares the raw HTML (200) with the real render and spots redirects only the browser runs. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does Google follow JavaScript redirects?
faq.a: Yes, but only after rendering the page, in a delayed second wave: it may index the wrong page in the meantime, and never sees the redirect if rendering fails.

faq.q: Do AI systems follow a JavaScript redirect?
faq.a: No: ChatGPT, Perplexity and the like read the raw HTML and barely run it; they see the original page, not the destination.

redirect_broken.constat: A page you had removed cleanly (redirect + removed from the sitemap) now returns a 4xx error: its redirect broke.
redirect_broken.pourquoi: As long as the redirect worked, the backlinks accumulated by the old URL passed their signals to the target — a 301 should stay in place for at least a year, and forever if external links still point to it. Once broken, the old address becomes a dead end: Google finds an error where it used to follow a path, and link equity stops flowing. The typical cause is silent: a routing refactor that deleted the redirect rule, or the deletion of the target page. Nobody visits these old URLs, so nobody notices — which is exactly why we monitor them.
redirect_broken.action: Restore the 301 redirect to the equivalent page still online (or to its new address if the target moved). The alert keeps the original target — no need to dig through your configuration to find it.
redirect_broken.gain: Signals from historical backlinks flow back into the right page instead of being lost on an error.
redirect_broken.seoTitle: Broken Redirect: Backlinks Falling into the Void
redirect_broken.h1: Broken redirect: an old URL now returns an error
redirect_broken.metaDescription: A cleanly removed page used to redirect; its redirect now returns a 4xx. Link equity from backlinks stops flowing. Often a silent refactor. How to fix it.
redirect_broken.tldr: A page you had removed cleanly (redirect + removed from the sitemap) now returns a 4xx error: its redirect broke and link equity from backlinks pointing to the old URL no longer flows. Restore the 301 redirect to the equivalent page still online.
whenNotAProblem: The old URL never received backlinks or traffic: the broken redirect has little real impact.
You now want to signal a deliberate permanent deletion with an explicit 410 rather than redirect.
The redirect target has legitimately disappeared and no equivalent page exists: a clean 410 is then consistent.

exemple.avant:
GET /old-product
-> HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
exemple.apres:
GET /old-product
-> HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: https://site.com/new-product
exemple.note: The alert keeps the redirect's original target: no need to dig through your configuration to find it.

actionSteps: Confirm the status the old URL returns (curl -I): does it answer a 4xx instead of redirecting?
Get the redirect's original target (shown in the alert) and check it is still online.
Restore the 301 redirect rule to that target, or to its new address if it moved.
Re-crawl the old URL to confirm it redirects cleanly again.
redirect_broken.scanHook: Do your old redirects still hold? Enter a URL: Seogard watches removed URLs and alerts you when a redirect that was passing backlinks starts returning an error. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: How long should a 301 redirect stay in place?
faq.a: At least a year, and ideally forever as long as external links still point to the old URL, to keep passing their signals.

faq.q: Why monitor URLs nobody visits anymore?
faq.a: Precisely because nobody visits them: a broken redirect goes unnoticed for months while it loses the benefit of historical backlinks.

rec_redirect_temporary.constat: A page removed from your sitemap redirects with a 302 or 307 — a redirect declared as temporary.
rec_redirect_temporary.pourquoi: A 302 tells Google "the content will come back here": it keeps the old URL indexed and is slower and more reluctant to consolidate signals toward the target. If the removal is actually permanent, this temporary status maintains an ambiguous state: the old address keeps occupying the index while the target does not fully receive the benefit of existing links. Google eventually treats a long-lived 302 as a 301, but that delay is neither documented nor guaranteed — better to send the right signal from the start.
rec_redirect_temporary.action: If the removal is permanent, switch the redirect to a 301 (permanent). Keep the 302 only if the original page is genuinely coming back.
rec_redirect_temporary.gain: The signal you send matches your actual intent: the old URL gets deindexed and links consolidate toward the target, with no period of ambiguity.
rec_redirect_temporary.seoTitle: 302 Redirect on a Removed Page: Prefer a 301
rec_redirect_temporary.h1: Temporary redirect (302) on a permanently removed page
rec_redirect_temporary.metaDescription: A page removed from the sitemap redirects with a temporary 302/307. Google keeps the old URL indexed longer. If the removal is permanent, switch to a 301.
rec_redirect_temporary.tldr: Minor recommendation: a page removed from your sitemap redirects with a 302/307 (temporary), so Google keeps the old URL indexed and consolidates signals toward the target more slowly. If the removal is permanent, switch the redirect to a 301 to send the right signal right away.
whenNotAProblem: The original page really is coming back: a 302 is then the right choice, exactly what it signals.
An accepted temporary redirect for a short operation (A/B test, maintenance, promo).
Google has already started treating your long-lived 302 as a 301: the residual impact is small.

exemple.avant:
GET /removed-page
-> HTTP/1.1 302 Found
Location: https://site.com/target-page
exemple.apres:
GET /removed-page
-> HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: https://site.com/target-page
exemple.note: 302 = temporary (the content will come back here); 301 = permanent (passes signals to the target). Choose based on your actual intent.

actionSteps: Check the exact redirect status (curl -I): 302 or 307?
Decide whether the removal is permanent, or the original page must genuinely return.
If permanent, replace the rule with a 301 (permanent); otherwise, keep the 302.
Re-crawl to confirm the new status returned.
rec_redirect_temporary.scanHook: Do your redirects send the right signal to Google? Enter a URL: Seogard reads the exact status of each redirect and spots the 302/307 on pages that are not coming back. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: What is the SEO difference between a 301 and a 302?
faq.a: A 301 is permanent and passes signals to the target while deindexing the old URL; a 302 is temporary and keeps the old URL indexed, expecting the content to return.

faq.q: Does Google eventually treat a 302 like a 301?
faq.a: Yes, if it persists long enough, but that delay is neither documented nor guaranteed; sending a 301 directly when the removal is permanent avoids that ambiguous period.

rec_unclean_removal.constat: A page removed from your sitemap returns a bare 404 — no redirect, no signal of permanent deletion.
rec_unclean_removal.pourquoi: The 404 works, but it is ambiguous: it says "not found", not "deleted on purpose". Google therefore keeps re-crawling the URL for weeks in case it comes back. And if backlinks pointed to this page, their value is lost on a dead end when a redirect would have passed it on. Two signals do better: 410 ("Gone") declares a deliberate deletion and speeds up deindexing; 301 preserves link equity when equivalent content exists. That said, an isolated 404 on a page with no history and no links has no real consequence — this recommendation matters mostly for pages that had value.
rec_unclean_removal.action: Permanent deletion — return 410. Content moved or an equivalent exists — 301-redirect to that page.
rec_unclean_removal.gain: Google stops crawling the dead URL sooner, and the value of existing links is preserved instead of being lost on a dead end.
rec_unclean_removal.h1: Removing a page cleanly: 410 or 301, never a bare 404
rec_unclean_removal.metaDescription: A deleted page that returns a bare 404 lets Google re-crawl it for weeks. When to return a 410, when to 301-redirect: the practical guide.
rec_unclean_removal.tldr: A bare 404 on a deleted page tells Google "not found", not "deleted on purpose": it keeps re-crawling for weeks and backlink value is lost. Return a 410 for a permanent deletion, or a 301 to an equivalent page if the content moved.
whenNotAProblem: The deleted page had no backlinks and no traffic history: a bare 404 is fine and this recommendation stays minor.
You already return a 410 (deletion) or a 301 (moved content): the signal is clean and there is nothing to fix.
The page is still intentionally in the sitemap and live: this is not a removal, it is an active page.

exemple.avant:
GET /old-product HTTP/1.1

HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
Content-Type: text/html
exemple.apres:
# Permanent deletion
HTTP/1.1 410 Gone

# or, if the content has an equivalent
HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: /new-product
exemple.note: 410 speeds up deindexing; 301 passes link value to the equivalent page. A bare 404 does neither.

actionSteps: Decide the intent: is the page gone for good, or does its content live elsewhere under a different URL?
Permanent deletion with no equivalent: configure the server to return a 410 Gone on this URL.
Moved or equivalent content: set a 301 redirect to the closest new URL (not to the homepage).
Remove the URL from the sitemap if not done yet, then re-crawl to confirm the new status code.
rec_unclean_removal.scanHook: Do your deleted pages return a bare 404 instead of a 410 or a 301? Enter a URL: Seogard tracks pages that leave your sitemap and alerts you when a removal leaves Google an ambiguous signal. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: What is the difference between a 404 and a 410 for Google?
faq.a: A 404 means "not found for now" and Google keeps re-crawling the URL to be safe; a 410 means "permanently gone" and speeds up deindexing.

faq.q: Should every deleted page be 301-redirected?
faq.a: No, only those whose content has a real equivalent. Redirecting a page with no equivalent to the homepage creates a soft 404: a clean 410 is better.

rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict.constat: This page is declared in your sitemap.xml but carries a noindex directive (robots meta tag, googlebot meta tag or X-Robots-Tag HTTP header).
rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict.pourquoi: The sitemap tells Google "this URL matters, index it" while the noindex says "ignore it". Google always sides with the noindex — the page will not be indexed — but it keeps re-crawling it since the sitemap presents it as important: crawl budget wasted on a page you exclude yourself. It is also one of the causes behind the Search Console "Excluded by noindex tag" report, which pollutes your indexing data with perfectly intentional exclusions. AI systems that read the sitemap as the site's inventory receive the same contradictory signal.
rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict.action: Pick a single intent: remove the page from the sitemap if it should stay unindexed (the noindex is then the only, consistent signal), or remove the noindex if it should rank.
rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict.gain: The sitemap becomes a reliable statement of intent again: Google focuses its crawl on the pages you actually want indexed, and Search Console reports stop mixing intentional exclusions with accidents.
rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict.seoTitle: Noindex page in your sitemap: the conflict to fix
rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict.h1: Sitemap and noindex on the same page: a contradictory signal
rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict.metaDescription: A page both in the sitemap and set to noindex asks Google to index it and ignore it. Wasted crawl budget, polluted GSC reports: how to settle it.
rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict.tldr: A page declared in the sitemap but carrying a noindex sends two opposite orders: Google obeys the noindex yet keeps re-crawling it, wasting budget and polluting your indexing reports. Pick one intent: remove it from the sitemap, or remove the noindex.
whenNotAProblem: The page just switched to noindex and is not yet removed from the sitemap: the conflict clears as soon as you update the sitemap.
The noindex was just removed on purpose to index the page: it rightfully belongs in the sitemap, no conflict left.
It is a deliberate temporary noindex (page under rework) that you are tracking and will lift soon.

exemple.avant:
<!-- sitemap.xml -->
<url><loc>https://site.com/page</loc></url>

<!-- /page in <head> -->
<meta name="robots" content="noindex">
exemple.apres:
<!-- Page to index: remove the noindex -->
<meta name="robots" content="index, follow">

<!-- OR page to exclude: drop it from the sitemap -->
<!-- (the URL no longer appears in sitemap.xml) -->
exemple.note: One single intent: either the page matters and stays indexable, or it is excluded and leaves the sitemap. Never both.

actionSteps: Decide whether this page should rank in Google or stay out of the index.
If it should rank: remove the noindex directive (robots/googlebot meta tag and X-Robots-Tag header).
If it should stay excluded: keep the noindex and remove its URL from sitemap.xml.
Re-crawl the page and check in Search Console that the "Excluded by noindex tag" conflict disappears.
rec_sitemap_noindex_conflict.scanHook: Is one of your pages both in the sitemap and set to noindex? Enter a URL: Seogard cross-checks your sitemap against each page's real indexing directives and alerts you the moment they contradict each other. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Will Google index a noindex page if it is in the sitemap?
faq.a: No. Facing a conflict, Google always honors the noindex: the page will not be indexed, but it keeps re-crawling it as long as the sitemap presents it.

faq.q: Where do I check a page's noindex directive?
faq.a: In the robots or googlebot meta tag in the HTML, and in the X-Robots-Tag HTTP header returned by the server: either one is enough to block indexing.

ssr_rendering_failed.constat: The HTML returned by your server is nearly empty, even though the page works in the browser.
ssr_rendering_failed.pourquoi: Google indexes the raw HTML first: it sees an empty page until the second rendering pass (24 hours to several weeks later). AI systems (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude) execute little or none of your JavaScript: to them, this page is nearly empty. This is the typical SSR failure that goes unnoticed because the site "works" visually.
ssr_rendering_failed.action: Fix the server-side rendering (SSR error, hydration, timeout) so the raw HTML contains the content.
ssr_rendering_failed.gain: Google indexes the real content on the first pass and AI systems can finally read and cite the page.
ssr_rendering_failed.seoTitle: Broken SSR: Google Indexes a Blank Page (Causes & Fix)
ssr_rendering_failed.h1: Broken SSR: when your server sends Google an empty page
ssr_rendering_failed.metaDescription: Broken server-side rendering returns near-empty HTML, so Google indexes a page with no content even though the site looks fine. Causes, detection and fix.
ssr_rendering_failed.tldr: Broken SSR means your server returns near-empty HTML, so Google indexes a page with no content even though the site renders fine in a browser. You need to fix server-side rendering so the content is present in the raw HTML, before any JavaScript runs.
whenNotAProblem: The page is intentionally client-rendered and not meant to be indexed (app interface, back office).
The main content is in the raw HTML and only a secondary part (widget, recommendations) is injected with JavaScript.
You are on the very first crawl of a new page: Seogard does not alert until it has a baseline.

exemple.apres:
Trail running shoes

Our selection of shoes…
exemple.note: On the left, all content depends on JavaScript. On the right, it is already in the raw HTML that Google and AI systems read first.

actionSteps: Open the page’s real source (right-click → "View page source", not the inspector): the main content should be there.
Find the server-side cause: rendering error, failed hydration, data timeout, or SPA fallback.
Fix server-side rendering so the response HTML contains the content, then redeploy.
Re-crawl the page to confirm the raw HTML is no longer empty.
ssr_rendering_failed.scanHook: Is your raw HTML empty on some pages? Enter a URL: Seogard compares what your server returns with what the browser renders, and tells you in 30 seconds whether Google sees a blank page. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: How do I know if my SSR is broken?
faq.a: View the page source (Ctrl+U) without running JavaScript: if the main content is missing, your server is returning an empty page to Google.

faq.q: Does Google eventually see JavaScript-rendered content?
faq.a: Yes, on its second rendering wave — often within seconds, but the delay can stretch to days or weeks depending on site size and crawl priority, and the render is never guaranteed. AI answer engines, on the other hand, almost never run it.

ssr_content_mismatch.constat: The raw HTML contains less than 10% of the content visible in the browser.
ssr_content_mismatch.pourquoi: Almost all the content is injected by JavaScript. Google sees a heavily stripped-down version on the first crawl, and LLMs — which read almost exclusively the raw HTML — capture close to nothing. Your content exists for humans but not for the machines that decide your visibility.
ssr_content_mismatch.action: Render the main content server-side (SSR/SSG) instead of injecting it client-side.
ssr_content_mismatch.gain: The full content becomes indexable on the first crawl and citable by AI systems.
ssr_content_mismatch.seoTitle: SSR/CSR gap: Google sees only 10% of the page
ssr_content_mismatch.h1: SSR/CSR gap: when the raw HTML holds almost nothing
ssr_content_mismatch.metaDescription: The HTML your server renders holds less than 10% of the content shown in the browser. Google and AI systems see a stripped page: causes and fix.
ssr_content_mismatch.tldr: An SSR/CSR gap means the raw HTML returned by the server holds less than 10% of the content visible in the browser: Google indexes a heavily stripped version and LLMs, which mostly read the raw HTML, capture almost nothing. You need to render the main content server-side instead of injecting it with JavaScript.
whenNotAProblem: The page is an app interface intentionally client-rendered (back office, dashboard) and not meant to be indexed.
The measured gap is on secondary content (recommendations, lazy-loaded comments) and the main body is in the raw HTML.
You are on the very first crawl of a new page: Seogard does not alert until it has a baseline.

exemple.apres:
Trail running guide

All the editorial content is already here...
exemple.note: On the left the server returns a shell and the content depends on JS. On the right it is present in the raw HTML that Google and AI systems read first.

actionSteps: View the real source (Ctrl+U, not the inspector) and compare it with what you see on screen: measure what is missing server-side.
Identify the content injected by JavaScript and why it does not come out of server rendering (client-side data, client-only component, SPA fallback).
Move the main content to server-side rendering (SSR) or pre-generation (SSG) so it appears in the HTML response.
Redeploy then re-crawl: the raw HTML should now contain most of the visible content.
ssr_content_mismatch.scanHook: How much of your content does Google actually see? Enter a URL: Seogard compares the raw HTML your server returns with the browser render and quantifies the gap in 30 seconds. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: How do I measure the gap between my raw HTML and the browser render?
faq.a: Compare the raw source (Ctrl+U, without running JS) to the rendered DOM in the inspector: if the raw is nearly empty, most of the page depends on JavaScript.

faq.q: Does Google eventually see JavaScript-injected content?
faq.a: Yes, on its second rendering pass, but with a delay from 24 hours to several weeks. AI answer engines almost never run it.

h1_missing.pourquoi: The H1 is a page's main topical signal for Google, and the anchor point LLMs use to understand what it is about. Its disappearance is almost always a template bug, not a choice.
h1_missing.action: Restore the H1 in the affected template.
h1_missing.gain: Google and AI systems can identify the page's main topic again.
h1_missing.seoTitle: H1 gone after a deploy: causes and fix
h1_missing.h1: My H1 disappeared after a release: what happened?
h1_missing.metaDescription: A page's H1 disappeared after a deploy. It is almost always a template bug, and Google loses the main topical signal. Diagnosis and fix.
h1_missing.tldr: An H1 vanishing after a deploy is almost always a template bug: Google and AI systems lose the main anchor that tells them what the page is about. You need to restore the H1 in the affected template, then re-crawl to confirm.
whenNotAProblem: The page was just redesigned and its main title is now carried, on purpose, by another element clearly marked as a heading.
It is a utility page with no editorial content (login page, redirect) where the absence of an H1 is intended.
You are on the very first crawl of the page: Seogard does not alert until it has a baseline version with an H1.

exemple.avant: ...
exemple.apres:
Trail running shoes for technical terrain

...
exemple.note: An empty title variable or a component removed by mistake is enough to drop the H1 across hundreds of pages at once.

actionSteps: Open the page source (Ctrl+U) and confirm no h1 tag is present, or that it is empty.
Find the template or component that outputs the main title and what changed in the last deploy (renamed variable, condition, empty data).
Restore a single, descriptive H1 that reflects the page's real topic.
Redeploy, re-crawl, and verify the H1 is present again in the raw HTML.
h1_missing.scanHook: Did your last deploy drop some H1 tags without you noticing? Enter a URL: Seogard compares the raw HTML from one crawl to the next and alerts you the moment a main title disappears. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Why did my H1 disappear after a release?
faq.a: Almost always a template bug: empty title variable, component removed by mistake, or a broken display condition during the deploy. Rarely an editorial choice.

faq.q: Is a missing H1 a Google penalty?
faq.a: It is not a direct penalty, but Google and AI systems lose the page's main topical signal, which can weaken how they understand and rank it.

h1_multiple.constat: The page contains multiple H1 tags.
h1_multiple.pourquoi: The topical signal gets diluted across several main titles. It is not a penalty, but Google and AI systems have to guess the real topic — and sometimes guess wrong. Often a component (logo, banner) wrongly marked up as an H1.
h1_multiple.action: Keep a single H1 (the editorial title) and demote the others to H2/H3 or non-heading tags.
h1_multiple.gain: One clear, unique topic per page — correct interpretation guaranteed.
h1_multiple.seoTitle: Multiple H1 tags on a page: is it a problem?
h1_multiple.h1: Multiple H1 tags on one page: what is the real impact?
h1_multiple.metaDescription: Multiple H1 tags on one page dilute the topical signal. It is not a penalty, but Google has to guess the real topic. How to get back to a single H1.
h1_multiple.tldr: Multiple H1 tags on a page do not trigger a penalty, but they dilute the topical signal and force Google and AI systems to guess the real topic, sometimes wrongly. Keep a single H1 (the editorial title) and demote the others to H2/H3.
whenNotAProblem: It is an HTML document using sections with their own H1 per the HTML5 spec, a deliberate and rare usage.
The extra H1 tags all carry the exact same editorial wording: the dilution is negligible, even if a single one is still preferable.
You are on the first crawl and want to confirm the second H1 is intentional before taking any action.

exemple.avant:
Brand logo

Trail running shoes

...
exemple.apres:
Brand logo

Trail running shoes

...
exemple.note: A logo or banner does not need to be an H1: only the editorial title deserves that level. A wrongly marked-up global component is the most common cause.

actionSteps: List every h1 tag on the page (search the source or audit via the inspector).
Identify the single title that truly sums up the page's editorial topic: that one keeps the H1.
Spot the stray H1 tags (logo, banner, widget) and demote them to H2/H3 or non-heading tags based on their role.
Redeploy and re-crawl to confirm only one H1 remains.
h1_multiple.scanHook: How many H1 tags do your pages actually expose? Enter a URL: Seogard reads the heading structure of your raw HTML and alerts you when a page carries more than one. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Are multiple H1 tags penalized by Google?
faq.a: No, it is not a penalty. But the topical signal gets diluted across several main titles and Google has to guess the real topic, which it sometimes gets wrong.

faq.q: Where do the extra H1 tags come from?
faq.a: Usually from a global component (logo, banner, header) wrongly marked up as an H1, which then ends up on every page of the site.

h1_changed.constat: The H1 text changed since the previous crawl.
h1_changed.pourquoi: If it is an editorial update, great. If it is a template bug (empty variable, wrong data), the rankings earned on the old wording can erode. Seogard flags the change so you can acknowledge it knowingly.
h1_changed.action: Check that the new H1 is intentional; otherwise fix the template.
h1_changed.gain: No topical change of direction goes unnoticed.
h1_changed.seoTitle: A page's H1 changed: intended or a bug?
h1_changed.h1: The H1 text changed: editorial update or accident?
h1_changed.metaDescription: A page's H1 text changed since the last crawl. Editorial or a template bug? How to check it before your rankings shift.
h1_changed.tldr: A changed H1 is fine if it is an intended editorial update, but if it is a template bug (empty variable, wrong data) the rankings earned on the old wording can erode. Check that the new H1 is intentional, otherwise fix the template.
whenNotAProblem: You deliberately reworded the page's editorial title: the change is intended, you just need to acknowledge the alert.
The change is cosmetic (punctuation, case, spacing) and does not alter the topic or the keywords the H1 carries.
The H1 embeds a legitimate dynamic value (price, date, stock) whose expected variation does not affect the topic.

exemple.avant: Trail running shoes for technical terrain
exemple.apres: Product page
exemple.note: An H1 dropping from rich wording to a generic label usually reveals an unset variable, not an editorial choice.

actionSteps: Compare the old and new H1 flagged by Seogard and decide whether the change is intended.
If it is intentional: acknowledge the alert, no fix is needed.
If it is accidental: find the source (title variable, data, template) and restore a descriptive, stable H1.
Redeploy if needed, then re-crawl to lock in the new baseline.
h1_changed.scanHook: Did a deploy change some H1 tags without you knowing? Enter a URL: Seogard compares the main title from one crawl to the next and shows you exactly what changed. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does changing an H1 text drop rankings?
faq.a: Not mechanically, but if the old wording carried the ranking for a query, replacing its keywords with a generic label can erode its relevance.

faq.q: How do I know if an H1 change is a bug?
faq.a: Look at the new text: a generic label ("Product page", empty, an ID) instead of a rich title almost always reveals an unset variable.

viewport_missing.pourquoi: Without a viewport, the page is no longer mobile-friendly. Google indexes mobile-first: a page unreadable on mobile is a poorly ranked page. It is almost always a layout/template that lost its tag during a deploy.
viewport_missing.action: Restore the viewport tag in the layout's head.
viewport_missing.gain: The page remains eligible for good mobile-first rankings.
viewport_missing.seoTitle: Viewport tag gone: the page breaks on mobile
viewport_missing.h1: Missing viewport tag: the page is no longer mobile-friendly
viewport_missing.metaDescription: The meta viewport tag disappeared from the head. The page is no longer responsive and Google indexes mobile-first. Bug causes and quick fix.
viewport_missing.tldr: Without the meta viewport tag the page is no longer mobile-friendly, and Google, which indexes mobile-first, ranks a page unreadable on phones poorly. It is almost always a layout that lost its tag during a deploy: restore it in the head.
whenNotAProblem: The page is not meant for mobile users or for indexing (internal tool, back office shown on desktop only).
The viewport was removed on purpose for a test and will be put back before the next release.
You are on the very first crawl of the page: Seogard does not alert until it has a baseline version with the tag.

exemple.avant:
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <title>Trail running shoes</title>
  <!-- viewport lost during the deploy -->
</head>
exemple.apres:
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <title>Trail running shoes</title>
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
</head>
exemple.note: Without this line, mobile shows the page at a scaled-down desktop width: tiny text, forced zoom, a broken experience that Google notices.

actionSteps: Open the page source (Ctrl+U) and confirm the meta name=viewport tag is missing from the head.
Find the layout or head template that lost the tag in the last deploy.
Restore the standard tag meta name=viewport content=width=device-width, initial-scale=1 in the head.
Redeploy, re-crawl, and check the mobile render (the browser's responsive inspection tool).
viewport_missing.scanHook: Did a deploy drop the viewport tag on your pages? Enter a URL: Seogard checks the head of your raw HTML on every crawl and alerts you the moment it disappears. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: What does the meta viewport tag do?
faq.a: It tells the browser to fit the page width to the device screen. Without it, mobile renders the page at desktop width, unreadable and poorly rated by Google.

faq.q: Why does the viewport tag disappear after a deploy?
faq.a: Almost always a modified layout or head component that lost the line: the tag then drops on every page sharing that template.

thin_content.constat: The page's content dropped below 200 words.
thin_content.pourquoi: Very short content gives Google little material to rank the page, and nothing usable for LLMs to cite. If the page used to have more content, a content component has probably stopped rendering.
thin_content.action: Check whether the thinness is normal (utility page) or restore the missing content.
thin_content.gain: The page becomes a real candidate for ranking and citation again.
thin_content.seoTitle: Thin content: the page drops below 200 words
thin_content.h1: Thin content: when a page no longer has enough material
thin_content.metaDescription: A page's content dropped below 200 words. Often a content component that stopped rendering. Impact on ranking and AI citation, plus the fix.
thin_content.tldr: Content dropping below 200 words gives Google little material to rank the page and nothing usable for LLMs to cite. If the page used to have more, a content component has often stopped rendering: check whether the thinness is normal or restore the missing content.
whenNotAProblem: It is a naturally short utility page (login, confirmation, contact) not meant to rank on content.
The page relies on media (video, interactive tool, gallery) whose value is not measured in word count.
You are on the first crawl of the page: Seogard has no prior length baseline to compare against.

exemple.avant: Trail running guide
exemple.apres:
Trail running guide

Introduction, detailed advice, sections... (full content restored)
exemple.note: A sharp drop in word count almost always signals a content block that stopped rendering, not an editorial choice.

actionSteps: Open the page and compare what you see with the expected content: is a whole block missing?
If the page used to have more, find the content component that stopped rendering (empty data, template error, content injected in JS only).
Restore the missing content, or confirm the thinness is normal for this type of page and acknowledge the alert.
Redeploy if needed, then re-crawl to confirm the content is present again in the raw HTML.
thin_content.scanHook: Did one of your pages lose its content without you noticing? Enter a URL: Seogard measures the content in your raw HTML on every crawl and alerts you when a page becomes too thin. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: How many words does a page need for Google?
faq.a: There is no official threshold: what matters is covering the topic. Below 200 words, most editorial pages lack the material to rank.

faq.q: Is thin content a Google penalty?
faq.a: Not an automatic penalty, but a very short page gives few relevance signals: it is less likely to rank and offers nothing usable for an AI citation.

content_removed.constat: More than 50% of the page's content is gone.
content_removed.pourquoi: A massive content drop is almost always an accident (broken component, wrong CMS data, deploy). Google recalculates the page's relevance on what remains — usually downward, and lastingly if nothing is fixed.
content_removed.action: Compare with the previous version and restore the missing content.
content_removed.gain: The page keeps the depth that justified its rankings.
content_removed.seoTitle: Content Removed: Why Your Page Lost Its Text (and Fix)
content_removed.h1: Content removed: when a page loses half its text
content_removed.metaDescription: More than half of a page's content vanished at once? Almost always a deploy bug. Causes, detection and how to restore the missing text.
content_removed.tldr: A content removed alert means more than 50% of a page's text disappeared between two crawls — almost always a broken component or template bug, not a decision. Compare with the previous version and restore the missing content before Google recalculates the page downward.
whenNotAProblem: You deliberately rewrote the page as a short version and the removed content was outdated.
The page merged into another and the text moved to a URL you redirect cleanly.
The main content is still there and only a secondary site-wide section (reviews, related products) was trimmed.

exemple.avant: Trail running guide
exemple.apres:
Trail running guide

1,200 words of detailed advice...
exemple.note: On the left the component that injects the article body renders nothing; on the right the content is back in the HTML.

actionSteps: Compare the page with its previous version to pinpoint exactly which block of text disappeared.
Trace the cause: broken component, empty CMS field, bad migration, or a missing content file.
Restore the content and redeploy.
Re-crawl the page to confirm the text volume is back to normal.
content_removed.scanHook: Did one of your pages lose its content without anyone noticing? Enter a URL: Seogard compares the text volume between crawls and alerts you when a page empties out. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Why would a page lose its content when nobody edited it?
faq.a: Content is usually injected by a component or CMS: a missing field, a broken component or a failed migration empties the page without changing its URL or template.

faq.q: Does Google downgrade a page that lost content?
faq.a: Google recalculates the page's relevance on the remaining text, usually downward; the effect can last as long as the content is not restored.

hreflang_removed.pourquoi: International targeting is broken: Google may serve the French version to a Spanish visitor, or let your language versions compete against each other. On a multilingual site, this is a major, silent regression.
hreflang_removed.action: Restore the hreflang block (generator or template) and check that the annotations are reciprocal.
hreflang_removed.gain: Each market gets the right language version in its search results.
hreflang_removed.h1: Hreflang removed: Google no longer knows which language to serve
hreflang_removed.metaDescription: All your hreflang tags vanished? Google may serve the wrong language and let your versions compete. Detection and how to restore them.
hreflang_removed.tldr: An hreflang removed alert means every tag that tells Google the language and country of each version has disappeared — international targeting breaks silently. Restore the hreflang block and make sure the annotations reference each other across all versions.
whenNotAProblem: Your site is monolingual and never needed hreflang.
You are intentionally consolidating several language versions into one and the removal is deliberate.
The tags moved to the HTTP header or the sitemap and are still present, just not in the HTML.

exemple.avant:
<!-- <head> -->
<!-- no hreflang tags left -->
exemple.apres:
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="fr" href="https://example.com/fr/" />
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="en" href="https://example.com/en/" />
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="x-default" href="https://example.com/" />
exemple.note: Each version must list all the others, including itself, with an x-default for languages that are not covered.

actionSteps: Confirm the removal is unintentional by comparing with a previous crawl.
Restore the hreflang block in the template or generator that emitted it.
Check reciprocity: each version must reference itself and every other version.
Re-crawl and check for hreflang errors in Search Console.
hreflang_removed.scanHook: Did your hreflang tags disappear after a deploy? Enter a URL: Seogard tracks your language annotations across crawls and warns you when international targeting breaks. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: What actually happens when hreflang disappears?
faq.a: Google loses the link between your language versions: it may show the wrong language in results or let two versions compete on the same query.

faq.q: Does hreflang have to be in the HTML?
faq.a: No, it can live in the HTTP header or the XML sitemap; what matters is that it is present somewhere and reciprocal across all versions.

hreflang_changed.constat: The languages declared in the hreflang tags changed.
hreflang_changed.pourquoi: Normal if you are adding/removing a language. If accidental, it can derail the targeting of an entire market. The change needs to be seen and validated.
hreflang_changed.action: Confirm that the new language list matches your strategy.
hreflang_changed.gain: International targeting stays under explicit control.
hreflang_changed.seoTitle: Hreflang Changed: Declared Languages Are Different
hreflang_changed.h1: Hreflang changed: your declared languages are no longer the same
hreflang_changed.metaDescription: The list of languages in your hreflang tags changed. Fine if you added a language, worth checking otherwise. How to verify and validate.
hreflang_changed.tldr: An hreflang changed alert flags that the list of languages declared in your tags changed — normal if you added or removed a language, worth fixing if it was accidental. Compare the new list against your strategy and validate the change.
whenNotAProblem: You just deliberately added or removed a language and the change reflects that decision.
You fixed a malformed language code (e.g. en-us to en-US) and the list is now valid.
A test or staging version was removed from the annotations, which is the intended effect.

exemple.avant:
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="fr" href="..." />
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="en" href="..." />
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="de" href="..." />
exemple.apres:
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="fr" href="..." />
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="en" href="..." />
exemple.note: The German version dropped off the list: intended if you are closing that market, accidental if it should have stayed.

actionSteps: Get the list of declared languages before and after the change.
Confirm it matches a product decision (language added or removed).
If it is accidental, restore the expected list in the template or generator.
Check the reciprocity of the annotations across all affected versions.
hreflang_changed.scanHook: Did your hreflang language list shift without you knowing? Enter a URL: Seogard tracks your language annotations and flags any change to validate. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Is an hreflang change always a problem?
faq.a: No: adding or removing a language changes the list legitimately. The risk only exists if the change is accidental and derails the targeting of a market.

faq.q: How do I know if the change was intentional?
faq.a: Compare the new language list against your roadmap: if a language disappeared with no matching decision, it is probably a template or deploy bug.

https_mixed_content.constat: Resources (images, scripts, styles) are loaded over HTTP on an HTTPS page.
https_mixed_content.pourquoi: Browsers block or flag these resources: broken padlock, missing elements, eroded user trust. Google prefers fully secure pages. Often an absolute URL forgotten in content or a template.
https_mixed_content.action: Switch the affected URLs to HTTPS (or to relative URLs).
https_mixed_content.gain: The padlock comes back, and no resources are blocked by the browser.
https_mixed_content.seoTitle: Mixed Content on HTTPS: Broken Padlock, Blocked Assets
https_mixed_content.h1: Mixed content: HTTP resources on an HTTPS page
