https_mixed_content.metaDescription: Images or scripts loaded over HTTP on an HTTPS page break the padlock and may be blocked by the browser. How to detect and fix mixed content.
https_mixed_content.tldr: A mixed content alert flags that resources (images, scripts, styles) load over HTTP on an HTTPS page: the security padlock breaks and browsers may block those elements. Switch the affected URLs to HTTPS or to relative URLs.
whenNotAProblem: It is an href hyperlink over HTTP (not a loaded resource): a link is not mixed content.
The resource comes from a third-party domain you do not control that already auto-redirects to HTTPS.
The HTTP call is in a comment or dead code the page never runs.

exemple.note: A plain https is enough; for internal assets, a relative URL (/assets/...) avoids hard-coding the protocol.

actionSteps: Open the browser console: it lists every mixed resource that is blocked or flagged.
Find where the HTTP URLs come from: CMS content, template, or a third-party script.
Switch those URLs to HTTPS, or to relative URLs for internal assets.
Reload the page and confirm the padlock is intact and no resource is blocked.
https_mixed_content.scanHook: Is a secure page still loading resources over HTTP? Enter a URL: Seogard inspects your pages and spots mixed content that breaks the padlock. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does mixed content really hurt my rankings?
faq.a: The direct ranking impact is small, but a broken padlock erodes visitor trust and mixed scripts are outright blocked, which can break page features.

faq.q: What is the difference between active and passive mixed content?
faq.a: Active resources (scripts, iframes) are blocked by default by browsers; passive ones (images) load but mark the page as not fully secure.

meta_description_missing.pourquoi: Google then generates a snippet from the content — often less compelling than your own copy. Impact is on CTR more than rankings: not critical, but clicks left on the table.
meta_description_missing.action: Restore the meta description in the template.
meta_description_missing.gain: You give Google your own copy for the snippet, which it shows when it deems it relevant (it frequently rewrites snippets based on the query).
meta_description_missing.seoTitle: Meta Description Removed: CTR Impact, Not Ranking
meta_description_missing.h1: Meta description removed: Google rewrites your snippet
meta_description_missing.metaDescription: With no meta description, Google generates an automatic snippet, often less compelling. No deindexing, but lost clicks. How to restore the tag.
meta_description_missing.tldr: A removed meta description causes no deindexing and no ranking loss: Google just generates an automatic snippet from the content, often less compelling than your copy. Restore the tag in the template to take back control of what makes people click.
whenNotAProblem: The page is not meant to capture organic clicks (utility page, confirmation, app interface).
You deliberately let Google generate the snippet because it fits highly varied queries better.
It is the very first crawl of the page: with no baseline, Seogard does not compare yet.

exemple.avant:
<head>
  <title>Trail running shoes</title>
  <!-- no meta description -->
</head>
exemple.apres:
<head>
  <title>Trail running shoes</title>
  <meta name="description" content="Our field-tested selection of trail running shoes, shipped in 48h.">
</head>
exemple.note: Aim for a factual, enticing sentence of about 150 characters, unique to each page.

actionSteps: Confirm the removal is unintentional by comparing with a previous crawl.
Find where the description came from: emptied CMS field, changed template, or a removed component.
Restore the meta description tag with unique, enticing copy for the page.
Re-crawl to confirm the tag is present again in the raw HTML.
meta_description_missing.scanHook: Did one of your pages lose its meta description after a deploy? Enter a URL: Seogard tracks your tags across crawls and alerts you when one disappears. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does losing my meta description drop my rankings?
faq.a: No: the meta description is not a ranking factor. It affects click-through rate in the results, not the page's position.

faq.q: Does Google always use my meta description?
faq.a: No, it frequently rewrites it based on the query; but providing yours gives it a polished option it shows when it is relevant.

structured_data_removed.constat: The page's structured data (JSON-LD) is gone.
structured_data_removed.pourquoi: You lose eligibility for rich results (stars, FAQ, products) in Google — a hard-earned CTR advantage that evaporates. AI systems also use this data to attribute and cite content: its disappearance makes you less readable to both engines.
structured_data_removed.action: Restore the deleted JSON-LD block (often a component removed by mistake).
structured_data_removed.gain: Rich results return and content becomes attributable by AI systems again.
structured_data_removed.seoTitle: Structured Data Removed: You Lose Your Rich Results
structured_data_removed.h1: Structured data removed: you lose your rich results
structured_data_removed.metaDescription: Your pages' JSON-LD disappeared: no more stars, prices or FAQ in Google, and content that is harder for AI to cite. How to restore it.
structured_data_removed.tldr: A structured data removed alert means the page's JSON-LD is gone: you lose eligibility for Google rich results (stars, prices, FAQ) and readability for AI. Restore the deleted block, often a component removed by mistake.
whenNotAProblem: The removed markup was faulty or ineligible and generated no rich results.
You deliberately moved the JSON-LD to another format or a canonical page.
The page targeted no rich result and the markup was purely decorative.

exemple.avant:
<head>
  <!-- Product JSON-LD block removed -->
</head>
exemple.apres:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Product",
  "name": "Trail shoe X",
  "offers": { "@type": "Offer", "price": "120", "priceCurrency": "EUR" }
}
</script>
exemple.note: The block tells Google and AI what the page is; without it, they have to guess everything from the text.

actionSteps: Compare with a previous crawl to confirm which markup type disappeared.
Trace the component or template that emitted the JSON-LD and restore it.
Validate the restored markup with Google's Rich Results Test.
Re-crawl to confirm the JSON-LD is back in the HTML.
structured_data_removed.scanHook: Did your structured data disappear after a release? Enter a URL: Seogard tracks your JSON-LD across crawls and alerts you when markup drops. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does losing JSON-LD lower my ranking?
faq.a: Structured data is not a direct ranking factor, but it drives the rich results that improve click-through rate, and helps AI attribute your content.

faq.q: Do AI systems really use structured data?
faq.a: Yes: JSON-LD gives them explicit facts (author, price, content type) that are easier to extract and cite than free text.

structured_data_error.constat: The page's JSON-LD is present but invalid (syntax error).
structured_data_error.pourquoi: Invalid JSON is simply ignored by Google and AI systems: all the benefits of structured data disappear while the code appears to be in place. The error often comes from an unescaped value injected dynamically.
structured_data_error.action: Fix the syntax (test with the Schema.org validator) and escape dynamic values.
structured_data_error.gain: Your structured data is actually consumed again, not just present.
structured_data_error.seoTitle: Invalid JSON-LD: Google Ignores Your Structured Data
structured_data_error.h1: Invalid JSON-LD: present but ignored by Google and AI
structured_data_error.metaDescription: Malformed JSON-LD is simply ignored: the code looks in place but produces no rich results. Common causes and how to fix the syntax.
structured_data_error.tldr: An invalid JSON-LD alert means your structured data is present but malformed: Google and AI ignore it, so no benefit despite code that looks in place. Fix the syntax and escape dynamic values.
whenNotAProblem: The broken block comes from a third-party widget or plugin you expect no rich result from.
It is a markup type not eligible for rich results, so its invalidity has no SERP stake.
You just fixed the syntax and are waiting for the next crawl to confirm the error is gone.

exemple.avant:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@type": "Product",
  "name": "Trail running shoe",
}
</script>
exemple.apres:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@type": "Product",
  "name": "Trail running shoe"
}
</script>
exemple.note: Here a trailing comma makes the whole block invalid; unescaped quotes in a dynamic value are the other common cause.

actionSteps: Paste the JSON-LD into Google's Rich Results Test or the Schema.org validator to locate the error.
Fix the faulty syntax: trailing comma, unclosed quote, or an unescaped character.
Always escape dynamic injected values (titles, descriptions) that contain quotes.
Re-crawl to confirm the markup is now valid and used.
structured_data_error.scanHook: Is your JSON-LD present but silently rejected? Enter a URL: Seogard parses your structured data and spots the invalid ones. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Why does my JSON-LD produce no rich result even though it is there?
faq.a: A single syntax error makes the whole block invalid: Google and AI ignore it entirely, even though the code appears in the page.

faq.q: Where do JSON-LD errors most often come from?
faq.a: From an unescaped dynamic value: a title or description containing a quote or apostrophe breaks the structure of the generated JSON.

og_image_removed.constat: The Open Graph image (og:image) is gone.
og_image_removed.pourquoi: No impact on Google rankings. But every share on LinkedIn, Slack, X or WhatsApp now displays without a visual — the click-through rate of shares drops. For a site that lives off social, this is far from cosmetic.
og_image_removed.action: Restore the og:image tag (and check that the image URL actually responds).
og_image_removed.gain: Your shared links become visual and clickable again.
og_image_removed.seoTitle: OG Image Removed: No Share Preview (But Not SEO)
og_image_removed.h1: OG image removed: your shared links lose their visual
og_image_removed.metaDescription: With no og:image, your shares on LinkedIn, Slack or WhatsApp show without a visual. No ranking impact, but lost social clicks. How to fix it.
og_image_removed.tldr: An og:image removed alert has no impact on Google rankings, but every share on LinkedIn, Slack, X or WhatsApp now shows without a visual — social clicks drop. Restore the og:image tag and check that the image URL responds.
whenNotAProblem: The page is never shared on social (utility page, interface, conversion funnel).
A site-wide fallback image takes over and a visual still shows.
You deliberately removed an outdated visual and the tag will be replaced shortly.

exemple.avant:
<head>
  <title>Trail running guide</title>
  <!-- no og:image tag -->
</head>
exemple.apres:
<head>
  <title>Trail running guide</title>
  <meta property="og:image" content="https://example.com/img/trail-guide.jpg">
</head>
exemple.note: Use an absolute URL and an image around 1200x630 px for a sharp preview on every platform.

actionSteps: Confirm the removal is unintentional by comparing with a previous crawl.
Restore the og:image tag in the template and point it to a valid absolute URL.
Check the image responds with 200 and matches the recommended ratio (about 1200x630 px).
Test the preview with a share debugger (LinkedIn Post Inspector, for example).
og_image_removed.scanHook: Do your shared links still show a visual? Enter a URL: Seogard tracks your og:image tag and alerts you when it disappears. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does removing og:image lower my rankings?
faq.a: No: og:image is not a ranking factor. Its only effect is on the preview shown when links are shared on social networks and messaging apps.

faq.q: Why does my share preview stay imageless even after fixing it?
faq.a: Platforms cache the old preview; force a refresh via their share debugger to reload the new image.

og_title_removed.constat: The Open Graph title (og:title) is gone.
og_title_removed.pourquoi: Without og:title, social platforms use the title tag or improvise. Impact limited to how shares render — not SEO — but it is a degraded storefront with a zero-cost fix.
og_title_removed.gain: Clean, controlled shares on every platform.
og_title_removed.seoTitle: og:title removed: your social shares (is it serious?)
og_title_removed.h1: og:title removed: when your social shares lose their title
og_title_removed.metaDescription: The og:title tag is gone, so social shares show an improvised or empty title. Social impact, not SEO. Here is how to restore it.
og_title_removed.tldr: og:title removed means your social shares no longer have a controlled title: platforms fall back to the title tag or improvise. It is a social-preview flaw, not a ranking issue, and it is fixed by restoring a single tag.
whenNotAProblem: The page is not meant to be shared on social networks (technical page, back office, checkout flow).
The page title is already perfect for sharing: platforms will use it when og:title is missing.
You are on the very first crawl of the page: Seogard does not alert until it has a baseline.

exemple.avant:
<head>
  <title>Trail running shoes - MySite</title>
  <!-- og:title missing -->
</head>
exemple.apres:
<head>
  <title>Trail running shoes - MySite</title>
  <meta property="og:title" content="Trail running shoes: our 2026 picks">
</head>
exemple.note: Without og:title, the social platform improvises from the title tag; with it, you control exactly what title shows in the share.

actionSteps: Open the page source and look for the meta property="og:title" tag.
Restore it in the head with a title written for sharing (compelling, not truncated).
Test the share preview with a network validator (Facebook Sharing Debugger, LinkedIn Post Inspector).
Re-crawl the page to confirm the tag is back.

faq.q: Is a missing og:title bad for SEO?
faq.a: No, Open Graph does not affect Google ranking. The impact is limited to how your shares look on social networks and messaging apps.

faq.q: What happens when og:title is missing?
faq.a: The social platform falls back to the title tag, or improvises from the content: you lose control of the title shown when the page is shared.

meta_refresh_detected.constat: A meta refresh redirect was detected.
meta_refresh_detected.pourquoi: It is the worst way to redirect: slower than a 301, passed less reliably by Google, and invisible to many tools. It often signals an old mechanism or a template hack.
meta_refresh_detected.action: Replace the meta refresh with a real server-side HTTP 301 redirect.
meta_refresh_detected.gain: The redirect passes the SEO signal cleanly and executes instantly.
meta_refresh_detected.seoTitle: Meta refresh detected: why replace it with a 301
meta_refresh_detected.h1: Meta refresh detected: the worst way to redirect a page
meta_refresh_detected.metaDescription: A meta refresh redirect is slower than a 301 and passed poorly by Google. Why it is a signal to fix and how to replace it with a proper redirect.
meta_refresh_detected.tldr: A meta refresh is a redirect inside the HTML: slower than a 301, passed less reliably by Google, and often invisible to tools. Replace it with a real server-side HTTP 301 redirect for a clean transfer of the signal.
whenNotAProblem: The meta refresh is an intentional reload of the same page (dashboard, status screen), not a redirect to another URL.
It is a temporary, non-indexed page (confirmation, internal app redirect) with no SEO stake.
You are on the very first crawl: Seogard sets a baseline before it alerts.

exemple.avant:
<head>
  <meta http-equiv="refresh" content="0; url=/new-page">
</head>
exemple.apres:
# Server-side redirect (HTTP header)
HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: /new-page
exemple.note: The meta refresh lives in the HTML and runs after load; the 301 is returned by the server first, and Google reads it as a true permanent move.

actionSteps: Find the meta http-equiv="refresh" tag and the target URL it points to.
Set up a server-side 301 redirect (server config, framework, or CDN) to that target.
Remove the meta refresh from the template once the 301 is in place.
Re-crawl to confirm the page returns a 301 and not a 200 with a meta refresh.
meta_refresh_detected.scanHook: Are old meta refresh redirects still lingering on your site? Enter a URL: Seogard analyzes the rendered HTML and detects meta refreshes that should be 301s. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does Google follow meta refresh redirects?
faq.a: Google eventually follows them, especially at delay 0, but treats them worse than a 301 and passes the signal less reliably. A server-side 301 remains the reference.

faq.q: What is the difference between meta refresh and 301?
faq.a: A 301 is returned by the server in the HTTP header, before any page; a meta refresh is an instruction inside the HTML, run after load, so it is slower and less reliable.

robots_txt_changed.pourquoi: One line of robots.txt can cut off crawling for entire sections. Most SEO deploy disasters (a staging "Disallow: /" pushed to production) trace back to this file. Every change deserves an immediate review.
robots_txt_changed.action: Review the robots.txt diff and confirm every modified rule.
robots_txt_changed.gain: No accidental crawl blocking survives more than one crawl.
robots_txt_changed.seoTitle: robots.txt changed: the file that can cut off your SEO
robots_txt_changed.h1: robots.txt changed: one line can block your entire crawl
robots_txt_changed.metaDescription: robots.txt changed: a single misplaced line cuts crawling for entire sections. Why to review every diff and how to check the file before Google reads it.
robots_txt_changed.tldr: robots.txt changed means the file that controls crawling of your site has changed: a line like Disallow: / pushed by mistake cuts indexing of entire sections. Review the diff right away and confirm every rule before Google reads it.
whenNotAProblem: The change is intentional and reviewed: adding a Sitemap line, deliberately blocking a technical directory.
The change only touches paths that are already non-indexed (sort parameters, cart, internal search).
You are on the very first crawl of the site: Seogard sets a baseline of the robots.txt first.

exemple.avant:
# robots.txt pushed by mistake (staging config)
User-agent: *
Disallow: /
exemple.apres:
# production robots.txt
User-agent: *
Disallow: /cart
Disallow: /search
Sitemap: https://mysite.com/sitemap.xml
exemple.note: Disallow: / blocks the whole site from crawling: it is the most classic SEO deploy disaster, often a staging file pushed as is.

actionSteps: Open the production robots.txt and compare it to the expected version, line by line.
Check first for a Disallow: / and for any blocks on your important sections.
Fix the source of the file (often an environment template) and redeploy.
Re-crawl and confirm the robots.txt serves the intended rules.
robots_txt_changed.scanHook: Did your robots.txt change on the last deploy? Enter a URL: Seogard watches the file on every crawl and alerts you the moment a line blocks crawling. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Can robots.txt deindex my pages?
faq.a: A Disallow prevents crawling but not always indexing. Combined with a rebuild, it can drop content from the index because Google can no longer revisit it. The risk is real.

faq.q: How often does Google read robots.txt?
faq.a: Google reloads robots.txt regularly, often cached for up to 24 hours. A bad file can therefore act with a slight delay, which is why fixing it fast matters.

heading_hierarchy_broken.constat: The heading hierarchy skips levels (e.g. H1 to H3 without an H2).
heading_hierarchy_broken.pourquoi: Honestly: the direct SEO impact is low. But a clean heading structure helps Google split the page into sections, LLMs extract answers, and screen readers navigate. It is hygiene at near-zero cost.
heading_hierarchy_broken.action: Restore the logical order of heading levels in the template.
heading_hierarchy_broken.gain: Clean extraction by machines and better accessibility, for a few minutes of work.
heading_hierarchy_broken.seoTitle: Broken heading hierarchy: is it bad for SEO?
heading_hierarchy_broken.h1: Broken heading hierarchy: when your H1, H2, H3 skip levels
heading_hierarchy_broken.metaDescription: A heading hierarchy that skips levels (H1 to H3) makes the page harder to segment. Low but real impact for machines and accessibility. How to fix it.
heading_hierarchy_broken.tldr: A broken heading hierarchy skips levels (for example H1 then H3 with no H2): the direct SEO impact is low, it is mostly hygiene. A clean structure helps Google and LLMs split the page into sections and screen readers navigate.
whenNotAProblem: The page has a single H1 followed by H2s, with no skipped level: the hierarchy is already correct.
The skip comes from a third-party, non-indexable component (widget, iframe) outside your main content.
You are on the very first crawl of the page: Seogard does not alert until it has a baseline.

exemple.avant:
Trail running guide

Choosing your shoes

Staying hydrated
exemple.apres:
Trail running guide

Choosing your shoes

Staying hydrated
exemple.note: On the left, it jumps from H1 to H3 with no H2; on the right, each section is an H2, giving a tree that machines can read.

actionSteps: List the page headings in order (a heading-structure extension is enough) and spot the skipped levels.
Fix the tags in the template so no level is skipped (H1 then H2 then H3).
Check that there is only one H1 per page.
Re-crawl the page to confirm the hierarchy is consistent.
heading_hierarchy_broken.scanHook: Do your headings skip levels on some pages? Enter a URL: Seogard analyzes the heading structure of the rendered HTML and shows you where the hierarchy breaks. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does an H1 followed by an H3 hurt SEO?
faq.a: There is no penalty. The direct ranking impact is low, but a clean structure helps Google and LLMs understand the page outline and improves accessibility.

faq.q: Should there be a single H1 per page?
faq.a: One clear H1 remains best practice: it states the main topic. Multiple H1s are not fatal but blur how the page is segmented.

lang_attribute_missing.constat: The lang attribute on the html tag is gone.
lang_attribute_missing.pourquoi: Moderate impact: Google detects the language from the content, but the attribute helps screen readers (pronunciation) and some parsers. It is a hygiene regression more than a ranking one.
lang_attribute_missing.action: Restore the lang attribute on the layout's html tag.
lang_attribute_missing.gain: Clean accessibility and an explicit language signal for every tool.
lang_attribute_missing.seoTitle: Lang attribute removed: SEO and accessibility impact
lang_attribute_missing.h1: Lang attribute removed: when the html tag loses its language
lang_attribute_missing.metaDescription: The lang attribute on the html tag is gone. Moderate SEO impact but real for screen readers and some parsers. Here is how to restore it.
lang_attribute_missing.tldr: Lang attribute removed means your html tag no longer declares the page language: Google infers it from the content, but screen readers and some parsers lose an explicit signal. It is a hygiene regression, fixed by restoring one attribute.
whenNotAProblem: The language is reliably declared elsewhere and the content is clearly monolingual: Google detects it easily.
The page is a technical, non-indexed screen not read by screen readers (back office, internal tool).
You are on the very first crawl of the page: Seogard does not alert until it has a baseline.

exemple.avant:
<!-- lang attribute missing -->
<html>
  <head>...</head>
exemple.apres:
<html lang="en">
  <head>...</head>
exemple.note: The lang attribute explicitly states the content language: essential for screen-reader pronunciation and useful for parsers.

actionSteps: Open the main layout and check the opening html tag.
Add the lang attribute with the correct language code (for example lang="en").
If the site is multilingual, make sure each version serves the right language code.
Re-crawl the page to confirm the lang attribute is present.

faq.q: Does the lang attribute affect Google ranking?
faq.a: The direct impact is moderate: Google mainly detects language from the content. The attribute is still a useful explicit signal, especially for accessibility and some parsers.

faq.q: What value should lang hold?
faq.a: A standard language code, for example en for English or fr for French. You can specify the region (en-US, fr-FR) but it is not required.

lang_attribute_changed.pourquoi: A lang switching from fr to en on a French site is almost always a default template deployed by mistake. Low direct impact, but a symptom of a layout modified without review.
lang_attribute_changed.action: Check whether it is intentional; restore the correct language otherwise.
lang_attribute_changed.gain: Language consistency preserved across the whole site.
lang_attribute_changed.seoTitle: Lang attribute changed: a default template deployed?
lang_attribute_changed.h1: Lang attribute changed: when the page language flips for no reason
lang_attribute_changed.metaDescription: The lang attribute on the html tag changed value. Often a default template deployed by mistake. Low direct impact, but a symptom worth checking.
lang_attribute_changed.tldr: Lang attribute changed means the declared page language switched value: on a French site, a lang flipping to en is almost always a default template deployed by mistake. The direct impact is low, but it is the symptom of a layout changed without review.
whenNotAProblem: The change is intentional: you fixed a wrong language code or genuinely translated the page.
You deliberately specified the region (fr became fr-FR) without changing the language.
You are on the very first crawl: Seogard sets a baseline of the lang attribute first.

exemple.avant:
<!-- French site, default template deployed -->
<html lang="en">
exemple.apres:

If it does not match, fix the attribute in the layout to restore the right code.
Find the source of the change (framework update, default template) to prevent a repeat.
Re-crawl the page to confirm lang serves the correct language.

faq.q: Does a wrong lang penalize SEO?
faq.a: The direct ranking impact is low. The real risk is an inconsistent language signal sent to screen readers and parsers, and a symptom of a layout changed by mistake.

faq.q: Why did my lang switch to en on its own?
faq.a: Most often, a framework or theme update shipped its default template, frequently with lang set to en, overwriting your original value.

word_count_changed.constat: The page's word count changed noticeably since the last crawl.
word_count_changed.pourquoi: A notable variation is often intentional (editorial update) — but it can signal a block that no longer renders. Low impact in itself: it is a monitoring signal, not a serious alert.
word_count_changed.action: Skim the page to confirm the variation is intentional.
word_count_changed.gain: You catch a template losing content blocks early.
word_count_changed.seoTitle: Content length changed: should you worry?
word_count_changed.h1: Content length changed: when a page's text varies sharply
word_count_changed.metaDescription: A page's word count changed sharply since the last crawl. Often intentional, sometimes a block that no longer renders. A monitoring signal to check.
word_count_changed.tldr: Content length changed is a monitoring signal, not a serious alert: a page's text varied noticeably since the last crawl. It is often an intended update, but sometimes a block that no longer renders. A quick look confirms the intent.
whenNotAProblem: The variation comes from an intended editorial update (expanded article, section added or removed).

If the variation is intended (editorial update), no action is needed.
If it is not, find the component that no longer renders (data, condition, JavaScript error).
Fix if needed, then re-crawl to set the new baseline length.

faq.q: Why does Seogard watch text volume?
faq.a: Because a sudden, unintended drop is often the first symptom of a rendering bug: a block that no longer shows, even before traffic moves.

charset_missing.pourquoi: Concrete risk: accents and special characters displayed incorrectly depending on the browser, and content potentially misparsed. Low direct SEO impact, but the display bug is immediate for visitors.
charset_missing.action: Restore the meta charset tag (UTF-8) at the very top of the head.
charset_missing.gain: Reliable text rendering across all browsers and parsers.
charset_missing.seoTitle: Charset removed: broken accents and garbled text
charset_missing.h1: Charset removed: when your special characters show up garbled
charset_missing.metaDescription: The encoding declaration (charset) is gone: accents and special characters may display incorrectly. Immediate visual impact, fixed with a single tag.
charset_missing.tldr: Charset removed means your page no longer declares its encoding: depending on the browser, accents and special characters can display wrong and content be misparsed. The direct SEO impact is low, but the display bug is immediate; you fix it with a tag at the top of the head.
whenNotAProblem: The server already sends an HTTP Content-Type header with charset=utf-8: the page is correctly encoded without the tag.
The page contains only pure ASCII (no accents or special characters): the bug is invisible.
You are on the very first crawl of the page: Seogard does not alert until it has a baseline.

exemple.avant:
<head>
  <title>Coffee and tea</title>
  <!-- meta charset missing -->
</head>
exemple.apres:
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <title>Coffee and tea</title>
</head>
exemple.note: The meta charset tag must sit at the very top of the head, before any text, so the browser decodes special characters correctly from the start.

actionSteps: Open the page source and look for the meta charset tag.
Restore it as the very first element of the head, with the value utf-8.
Make sure no text or other tag precedes it in the head.
Re-crawl the page and check that accents and special characters display correctly.
charset_missing.scanHook: Do your special characters display correctly after a deploy? Enter a URL: Seogard checks the charset declaration in the rendered HTML and alerts you if it disappears. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Is a missing charset bad for SEO?
faq.a: The direct ranking impact is low, especially if the server sends the encoding via the HTTP header. The real risk is broken character display for your visitors.

faq.q: What charset value should I use?
faq.a: utf-8 in almost every case: it is the universal encoding that handles accents, languages, and emojis. The tag must sit at the very top of the head.

ssr_title_mismatch.constat: The title in the raw HTML differs from the one displayed after JavaScript executes.
ssr_title_mismatch.pourquoi: Google may index the raw version's title (often a generic one like "App"), not the one you think you have. LLMs see almost only the raw title. You are optimizing around a title the machines never read.
ssr_title_mismatch.action: Set the title server-side (SSR head), not via a client-side script.
ssr_title_mismatch.gain: The title that gets indexed and cited finally matches the one you wrote.
ssr_title_mismatch.seoTitle: SSR vs CSR Title Mismatch: Google Indexes the Wrong One
ssr_title_mismatch.h1: Different title in raw HTML and after JavaScript: which one does Google read?
ssr_title_mismatch.metaDescription: When the raw HTML title differs from the JavaScript-rendered one, Google indexes the raw version, often a generic title. Detection and fix.
ssr_title_mismatch.tldr: Your raw HTML title is not the same as the one shown after JavaScript runs: Google and AI systems read the raw title, often a generic fallback. You need to set the title server-side so the indexed version is the one you actually wrote.
whenNotAProblem: The raw HTML title is already correct and the JavaScript version just repeats it identically: no real gap.
The page is not meant to be indexed or cited (app interface, logged-in screen): the title Google sees barely matters.
The gap is only a difference in spacing or casing with no change in meaning: cosmetic, with no SEO impact.

exemple.avant:
<!-- Raw HTML returned by the server -->
<title>App</title>
<!-- after JavaScript, in the browser -->
<title>Men's trail running shoes - Brand</title>
exemple.apres:
<!-- Raw HTML returned by the server -->
<title>Men's trail running shoes - Brand</title>
exemple.note: On the left, Google indexes "App". On the right, the real title is present in the raw HTML, before any JavaScript runs.

actionSteps: View the page's real source (right-click then View page source, not the inspector) and read the <title> tag as it arrives from the server.
Find the client component that rewrites the title afterwards (SPA router, browser-side head hook).
Move the title definition into server-side rendering (SSR head) so it is correct in the HTTP response itself.
Re-crawl the page and confirm the raw title now matches the displayed title.
ssr_title_mismatch.scanHook: Is the title Google indexes really the one you wrote? Enter a URL: Seogard compares the raw HTML title with the JavaScript-rendered one and shows you the gap in 30 seconds. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does Google eventually see the JavaScript-rendered title?
faq.a: It may see it on its second rendering pass, but it has already indexed the raw title in the meantime, and AI answer engines almost only read the raw version.

faq.q: How do I know which title is actually indexed?
faq.a: Look at the title in the source (Ctrl+U) without running JavaScript: that is the one Google prioritizes, not the one in the browser tab.

ssr_meta_description_mismatch.constat: The meta description in the raw HTML differs from the one rendered by JavaScript.
ssr_meta_description_mismatch.pourquoi: Google often bases the results snippet on the raw version; AI systems read only that one. The description you optimized is probably never seen by the machines.
ssr_meta_description_mismatch.gain: The description read by Google and AI systems is the one you actually wrote, not a phantom text (Google remains free to rewrite the displayed snippet based on the query).
ssr_meta_description_mismatch.seoTitle: SSR vs CSR Meta Description Mismatch: Google Reads the Raw One
ssr_meta_description_mismatch.h1: Meta description differs in raw HTML and after JavaScript
ssr_meta_description_mismatch.metaDescription: When the raw HTML meta description differs from the JavaScript-rendered one, Google and AI systems read the raw version. How to detect and fix the gap.
ssr_meta_description_mismatch.tldr: Your raw HTML meta description is not the one rendered after JavaScript: Google bases the snippet on the raw version and AI systems only read that one. You need to render the meta description server-side so it is correct in the raw HTML.
whenNotAProblem: The raw meta description is already correct and JavaScript just reproduces it: no useful gap to fix.
The page is not meant for search (app screen, technical page): the snippet barely matters.
The gap comes down to punctuation or a space, without changing the meaning: cosmetic.

exemple.avant:
<!-- Raw HTML returned by the server -->
<meta name="description" content="">
<!-- after JavaScript -->
<meta name="description" content="Our selection of trail running shoes tested in the field.">
exemple.apres:
<!-- Raw HTML returned by the server -->
<meta name="description" content="Our selection of trail running shoes tested in the field.">
exemple.note: On the left, the raw HTML has an empty description and Google builds a snippet at random. On the right, the intended description is present in the server response.

actionSteps: View the page's real source (Ctrl+U) and read the meta description tag as it arrives from the server.
Find the client script that injects or replaces the description after load.
Move the meta description definition into server-side rendering (SSR head).
Re-crawl the page to confirm the raw description is now the correct one.
ssr_meta_description_mismatch.scanHook: Is the description Google reads the one you wrote? Enter a URL: Seogard compares the raw HTML meta description with the JavaScript-rendered one. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Google often rewrites the snippet anyway, is this really useful?
faq.a: Google stays free to rewrite the snippet based on the query, but it starts from your raw meta: an empty or phantom description gives it less to work with, and AI systems only have that version.

faq.q: Which version of the description does Google use?
faq.a: It relies first on the meta present in the raw HTML; the one injected later in JavaScript may never be taken into account.

meta_title_changed.constat: The meta title changed since the previous crawl.
meta_title_changed.pourquoi: Intentional? Fine. Accidental (empty variable, generic fallback)? The CTR and rankings earned on the old title can erode silently. The change deserves to be seen and acknowledged, not discovered three weeks later.
meta_title_changed.action: Confirm that the new title is intentional; otherwise fix it.
meta_title_changed.gain: No unintended rewrite of your titles reaches production without validation.
meta_title_changed.seoTitle: Meta Title Changed: Intentional or Template Bug?
meta_title_changed.h1: Your meta title changed: intended update or silent regression?
meta_title_changed.metaDescription: A changing meta title may be a legitimate A/B test or a generic fallback shipped by mistake. How to verify intent and protect your CTR.
meta_title_changed.tldr: Your meta title changed since the previous crawl: healthy if it is an editorial choice, risky if it is a generic fallback shipped by mistake that erodes CTR and earned rankings. You need to confirm the new title is intentional, otherwise fix it.
whenNotAProblem: You deliberately reworked the title (rebrand, A/B test, new hook): the change is intended and documented.
The change follows a normal page evolution (new year, updated price in the title): it is healthy editorial work.
The gap is purely cosmetic (separator, space) and changes neither the meaning nor the keywords of the title.

exemple.avant:
<!-- after a deploy -->
<title>Home</title>
exemple.apres:
<!-- expected title -->
<title>Men's trail running shoes - 48h delivery - Brand</title>

actionSteps: Compare the old and new title provided by the alert and ask whether someone deliberately made this change.
If it is intended, acknowledge the alert: it documents the change in the history.
If nobody changed anything, find the cause (empty variable, template fallback, missing data) and fix it.
Redeploy then re-crawl to confirm the expected title is back.
meta_title_changed.scanHook: Did a deploy rewrite your titles without anyone noticing? Enter a URL: Seogard compares the title across crawls and alerts you on any change. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does changing a title lose rankings?
faq.a: An intended, coherent change, no: Google recomputes relevance. A generic fallback shipped by mistake, however, can erode CTR and the positions earned on the old title.

faq.q: Why get alerted if the change was intentional?
faq.a: Because a deliberate change and an accidental regression are indistinguishable without you: the alert lets you acknowledge what is intended in one second and catch the rest.

meta_description_changed.constat: The meta description changed since the previous crawl.
meta_description_changed.pourquoi: Like the title: healthy if editorial, suspicious if nobody changed anything. Moderate impact (CTR), but a generic fallback deployed by mistake across an entire template deserves to be caught fast.
meta_description_changed.action: Check whether the change is intentional.
meta_description_changed.gain: Snippet regressions are detected on the next crawl, not in the monthly report.
meta_description_changed.seoTitle: Meta Description Changed: Should You Worry?
meta_description_changed.h1: Your meta description changed: most of the time, it is nothing
meta_description_changed.metaDescription: A changing meta description is rarely a problem: usually an editorial update. The real risk is a generic fallback shipped across an entire template.
meta_description_changed.tldr: Your meta description changed since the previous crawl: this is a minor signal, most often a healthy editorial update. The only case to watch is a generic fallback shipped by mistake across a whole template, so just confirm the change is intended.
whenNotAProblem: You or the content team edited the description: this is normal editorial work, no action needed.
The change affects a single page following an update to its content: coherent and expected.
Google often rewrites the snippet by query anyway: a minor variation has little real effect.

exemple.avant:
<!-- after a deploy, across hundreds of pages -->
<meta name="description" content="Welcome to our site.">
exemple.apres:
<!-- expected description, specific to the page -->
<meta name="description" content="Men's trail running shoes tested over 300 km: our comparison and advice.">
exemple.note: The same generic description suddenly appearing across a whole template is the signal to catch; an isolated editorial change is not.

actionSteps: Look at the old and new description provided by the alert to judge whether the change is editorial.
If it is intended, acknowledge: the alert stays in the history with no further action.
If the same generic description appeared across many pages, find the template fallback responsible.
Fix the template then re-crawl to confirm descriptions are unique to each page again.
meta_description_changed.scanHook: Did a deploy overwrite your descriptions with generic text? Enter a URL: Seogard tracks the meta description across crawls and warns you on change. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does changing a meta description drop traffic?
faq.a: Rarely on its own: the impact is moderate and mostly on CTR. The real risk is an empty or generic fallback shipped at scale, which weakens all your snippets at once.

faq.q: Why does Seogard rank this alert as mere info?
faq.a: Because a description change is most often harmless and Google often rewrites the snippet: we flag it to catch accidental deploys, without overdramatizing.

ssr_blocked.constat: Your firewall (WAF/anti-bot) blocked Seogard's HTTP request and returned a challenge page.
ssr_blocked.pourquoi: If one legitimate crawler is blocked, others may be too: AI citation crawlers, SEO tools, sometimes even some Google fetches. At minimum, your monitoring is blind on these pages.
ssr_blocked.action: Allow legitimate crawlers (including Seogard) in your WAF/CDN configuration.
ssr_blocked.gain: Monitoring resumes, and you avoid silently blocking bots that help your visibility.
ssr_blocked.seoTitle: Crawler Blocked by Anti-Bot: What This Signal Means
ssr_blocked.h1: Seogard blocked by your firewall: a blind spot to fix
ssr_blocked.metaDescription: When a WAF (Cloudflare, Akamai) returns a challenge page instead of content, monitoring is blind on that page. Not an SEO regression, but worth fixing.
ssr_blocked.tldr: This signal is not an SEO regression of your page: your anti-bot firewall returned a challenge page instead of the content, so this crawl's data is not reliable. You need to allow legitimate crawlers in your WAF so monitoring becomes reliable again.
whenNotAProblem: You intentionally block all external crawlers on a private or staging area: the block is deliberate, not a surprise.
The message is one-off and gone on the next crawl: the WAF weathered a load spike, with no lasting block.
The page in question is not meant to be indexed or cited: the monitoring blind spot has no consequence.

exemple.avant:
# The WAF returns a challenge page to the fetcher
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: cloudflare
<title>Just a moment...</title>
<!-- 858 bytes, no real content -->
exemple.apres:
# WAF rule: allow the monitoring crawler
# (allowlist by User-Agent or by IP range)
Allow: Seogard monitoring bot
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
<main>... real page content ...</main>
exemple.note: It is not your SEO breaking: it is the HTTP fetcher getting a challenge instead of the HTML. Fixing it restores measurement, it does not repair the page.

actionSteps: Open your WAF/CDN configuration (Cloudflare, Akamai, etc.) and find the anti-bot rules applied to the URL in question.
Add the monitoring crawler to the allowlist, by User-Agent or by IP range.
Check that other useful bots (AI citation crawlers, other SEO tools) are not blocked by the same overly broad rule.
Re-run a crawl and confirm the page now returns its real content, not a challenge.
ssr_blocked.scanHook: Is your firewall also blocking bots useful to your visibility? Enter a URL: Seogard attempts the HTTP fetch and tells you if an anti-bot challenge answers instead of the content. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does this message mean my page has an SEO problem?
faq.a: No: it is a measurement blind spot, not a page regression. The WAF blocked the crawler, so this crawl's data is discarded; the page may be perfectly healthy for Google.

faq.q: If Seogard is blocked, is Googlebot blocked too?
faq.a: Not necessarily, but an overly aggressive WAF that blocks a legitimate crawler can also hinder AI citation crawlers or some fetches: that is the real risk to check.

llms_txt_removed.constat: The /llms.txt file was deleted from the site.
llms_txt_removed.pourquoi: Honestly: low impact to date — llms.txt is a convention that major LLMs do not confirm consuming. We flag the deletion because you had set it up deliberately: if it is accidental, it is a symptom of a deploy that may have wiped other static files too.
llms_txt_removed.action: Check that the deletion is intentional; otherwise restore the file and audit the deploy.
llms_txt_removed.gain: Consistency with your original intent, and confirmation that the deploy did not wipe anything else.
llms_txt_removed.h1: The /llms.txt file is gone: real impact and what it reveals
llms_txt_removed.metaDescription: Removing /llms.txt has low impact today: no major LLM confirms reading it. The signal's real value is detecting a deploy that wiped static files.
llms_txt_removed.tldr: Let us be honest: removing /llms.txt has low impact today, because no major LLM confirms consuming it. We flag it mainly because if it is accidental, it is a symptom of a deploy that may have wiped other static files, so confirm it is intended.
whenNotAProblem: You knowingly removed /llms.txt because you no longer maintain it: a deliberate decision, no action needed.
You never relied on this file: its absence changes nothing about your real SEO or AI visibility.
The rest of your deploy is intact (robots.txt, sitemap, assets present): the removal is isolated and has no side effects.

exemple.avant:
# https://example.com/llms.txt
# (file missing: 404)
exemple.apres:
# https://example.com/llms.txt
# Example - documentation for agents
> Site summary and links to key pages.

## Docs
- [Guide](https://example.com/guide)
exemple.note: The file is optional: its real value is documenting the site for agents. Its absence does not exclude your site from AI answers.

actionSteps: Check whether removing /llms.txt was intentional or nobody decided it.
If it is accidental, verify the same deploy did not also remove robots.txt, the sitemap or other static files.
Restore the file if you want to keep it, or acknowledge the alert if the removal is deliberate.
Re-crawl to confirm the final state and close the signal.
llms_txt_removed.scanHook: Did a deploy wipe static files without you noticing? Enter a URL: Seogard tracks the presence of /llms.txt and root-level signals across crawls. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does removing /llms.txt drop me from ChatGPT or Perplexity answers?
faq.a: No: no major LLM confirms reading this file today. What matters to be cited is that your pages stay accessible to citation crawlers, not the presence of llms.txt.

faq.q: Why get alerted about a low-impact removal?
faq.a: Because the file was set up deliberately: an unplanned disappearance often reveals a deploy that erased other, more important files.

ai_crawlers_blocked_changed.constat: The robots.txt now blocks AI citation crawlers that were previously allowed.
ai_crawlers_blocked_changed.pourquoi: Citation crawlers (OAI-SearchBot and ChatGPT-User for OpenAI, PerplexityBot and Perplexity-User for Perplexity, Claude-SearchBot and Claude-User for Anthropic) are the ones that let ChatGPT search, Perplexity or Claude consult and CITE your pages. Blocking them removes you from AI answers. Not to be confused with training crawlers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, Google-Extended), whose blocking does not affect citation.
ai_crawlers_blocked_changed.action: Remove the citation crawlers from the robots.txt Disallow rules (keep blocking training bots if that is your policy).
ai_crawlers_blocked_changed.gain: Your content becomes consultable and citable by AI answer engines again.
ai_crawlers_blocked_changed.seoTitle: robots.txt Blocks AI Citation Crawlers: The Impact
ai_crawlers_blocked_changed.h1: You just blocked AI citation crawlers: what does it do?
ai_crawlers_blocked_changed.metaDescription: Blocking OAI-SearchBot, PerplexityBot or Claude-SearchBot removes your pages from ChatGPT search, Perplexity and Claude answers. Different from training bots.
ai_crawlers_blocked_changed.tldr: Your robots.txt now blocks AI citation crawlers (OAI-SearchBot, PerplexityBot, Claude-SearchBot...): these are the ones that let ChatGPT search, Perplexity and Claude consult and cite your pages. You need to remove them from the Disallow rules if you want to stay citable.
whenNotAProblem: You knowingly decided to opt out of AI answer engines: the block is a deliberate policy.
The bots involved are training crawlers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, Google-Extended): blocking them does not affect citation, only model training.
The block targets a private or paywalled area not meant to be cited: consistent with your strategy.

exemple.avant:
# robots.txt
User-agent: OAI-SearchBot
Disallow: /

User-agent: PerplexityBot
Disallow: /
exemple.apres:
# robots.txt
User-agent: OAI-SearchBot
Allow: /

User-agent: PerplexityBot
Allow: /

# You may keep Disallow on training bots if needed
User-agent: GPTBot
Disallow: /
exemple.note: Citation crawlers (live search/browse) grant access to AI answers; training crawlers do not. The two are distinct.

actionSteps: Open your robots.txt and find the new Disallow rules targeting citation crawlers (OAI-SearchBot, ChatGPT-User, PerplexityBot, Perplexity-User, Claude-SearchBot, Claude-User).
Determine whether this block is intended or inherited from an overly broad User-agent: * rule.
Remove these citation crawlers from the Disallow rules while keeping, if that is your policy, the block on training bots.
Redeploy the robots.txt then re-crawl to confirm the citation crawlers are allowed again.
ai_crawlers_blocked_changed.scanHook: Did a deploy block the bots that cite you in ChatGPT or Perplexity? Enter a URL: Seogard reads your robots.txt and tells citation from training crawlers apart. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: What is the difference between a citation crawler and a training crawler?
faq.a: A citation crawler (OAI-SearchBot, PerplexityBot, Claude-SearchBot) reads your pages live to answer and cite you; a training crawler (GPTBot, ClaudeBot) collects text to train a model. Blocking the latter does not affect citation.

faq.q: Does blocking these crawlers have an immediate effect?
faq.a: Yes, on their next attempt: a blocked crawler can no longer read your pages, so AI answer engines can no longer consult and cite them.

robots_blocks_googlebot.constat: The robots.txt blocks Googlebot on part of the site.
robots_blocks_googlebot.pourquoi: This is the most radical incident possible: the blocked sections will no longer be crawled, so no longer updated in the index, then degraded. The staging "Disallow" pushed to production is a classic cause of massive traffic losses.
robots_blocks_googlebot.action: Immediately remove the rule blocking Googlebot (unless the blocking is deliberate and documented).
robots_blocks_googlebot.gain: Google keeps access to everything that should be indexed.
robots_blocks_googlebot.seoTitle: robots.txt Blocks Googlebot: Critical Incident to Fix
robots_blocks_googlebot.h1: Your robots.txt blocks Googlebot: the deindexing that is coming
robots_blocks_googlebot.metaDescription: A Disallow on Googlebot stops crawling of a whole section: no more index updates, then deindexing. The classic staging-to-prod mistake. Fix urgently.
robots_blocks_googlebot.tldr: Your robots.txt blocks Googlebot on part of the site: this is the most radical incident possible, the blocked sections are no longer crawled, no longer updated in the index, then degrade. You need to remove the Disallow rule immediately, unless the block is deliberate and documented.
whenNotAProblem: The block is deliberate and documented on an area that should not be indexed (back office, cart, internal search results).
The blocked path contains no page meant for search: no organic traffic is at stake.
It is a staging site that must stay out of the index: a global Disallow is exactly what you want there.

exemple.avant:
# robots.txt (staging pushed to prod by mistake)
User-agent: *
Disallow: /
exemple.apres:
# robots.txt
User-agent: *
Allow: /

# Block only what does not need indexing
Disallow: /cart
Disallow: /admin
exemple.note: "Disallow: /" under User-agent: * cuts Google off from the whole site: the line to never let reach production.

actionSteps: Open the production robots.txt and find the Disallow rule that applies to Googlebot (under User-agent: Googlebot or User-agent: *).
Confirm this block is not intended: in almost all cases, a broad Disallow on Google is an accident (staging pushed to prod).
Remove or narrow the rule so it only blocks paths that truly should not be indexed, then redeploy immediately.
Re-crawl and, in Search Console, test the robots.txt to confirm Googlebot can access the pages to be indexed again.
robots_blocks_googlebot.scanHook: Is your robots.txt blocking Google without you knowing? Enter a URL: Seogard reads your robots.txt and alerts you on the first Disallow that cuts off Googlebot. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: How long before deindexing if Googlebot is blocked?
faq.a: Crawling stops at once on the blocked paths; the index stops being refreshed, then pages degrade and eventually drop out of results. The longer the block lasts, the deeper the loss.

faq.q: Is blocking in robots.txt enough to deindex a page?
faq.a: No, and it is a trap: a page already indexed but blocked from crawling can stay in the index while Google cannot see a noindex. To deindex cleanly, let Google crawl the page and serve it a noindex.

faq_schema_removed.constat: The page's FAQPage schema is gone.
faq_schema_removed.pourquoi: Google no longer shows FAQ rich results in the SERP: the FAQPage schema therefore no longer brings any enhanced display in search results, and its removal has no ranking impact. The visible question/answer content, however, remains readable by Google and LLMs — which read the page's text, not the markup. We flag the removal because it is often a symptom of a component deleted by mistake.
faq_schema_removed.action: Check that the removal is intentional; restore the component if it is not.
faq_schema_removed.gain: Confirmation that the removal is deliberate, not a side effect of a deploy — the schema alone carried no traffic.
faq_schema_removed.seoTitle: FAQ Schema Removed: Does It Hurt Your SEO?
faq_schema_removed.h1: FAQPage schema removed: what it really changes for your results
faq_schema_removed.metaDescription: The FAQPage schema is gone from your page. Google no longer shows the FAQ rich result, but your Q&A content stays readable. Real impact, likely cause and fix.
faq_schema_removed.tldr: Losing the FAQPage schema removes the enhanced FAQ display in Google, but it does not affect ranking or how Google and AI systems read your questions and answers. We flag it mainly because it is often the symptom of a component removed by mistake: confirm the removal was intended.
whenNotAProblem: You deliberately removed the FAQ from the page because it no longer belonged there.
The visible question/answer content is still present: Google and AI systems read it in the text, regardless of the markup.
The page never had a truly relevant FAQ and the schema produced no usable rich result anyway.

exemple.avant:
Frequently asked questions

Delivery within 48h ...
exemple.apres:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [{
    "@type": "Question",
    "name": "What are the delivery times?",
    "acceptedAnswer": { "@type": "Answer", "text": "Delivery within 48h." }
  }]
}
</script>
exemple.note: The visible content stays readable by every engine; the schema only turns on the enhanced SERP display.

actionSteps: Check the raw HTML to see whether the FAQPage JSON-LD block is truly gone or just modified.
Decide whether the removal is intentional or a side effect of a recent deploy.
If it was not intended, restore the component that injected the FAQPage schema.
Re-crawl the page to confirm the schema is present again.
faq_schema_removed.scanHook: Has your FAQ schema vanished without you noticing? Enter a URL: Seogard compares the raw HTML from one crawl to the next and spots removed FAQPage markup. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does removing the FAQ schema drop my traffic?
faq.a: No, the FAQPage schema carries no traffic: it only enables the enhanced FAQ display in the SERP. Your ranking and content stay unchanged.

faq.q: Does Google still show FAQ rich results?
faq.a: Google has heavily restricted FAQ display to authoritative and government sites. For most sites this rich result no longer appears, which limits how much this schema matters.

structured_data_author_removed.constat: The author field disappeared from the structured data.
structured_data_author_removed.pourquoi: Attribution (who wrote this) is a trust signal in Google's E-E-A-T logic and a reference point for AI systems when they decide what to cite. Nothing guarantees that an author field alone triggers a citation, but attributed content starts with one more signal than anonymous content. Moderate impact on citability, low on classic rankings.
structured_data_author_removed.action: Restore the author field in the JSON-LD.
structured_data_author_removed.gain: Your content remains attributable — a criterion generative engines value.
structured_data_author_removed.seoTitle: Author Removed from JSON-LD: E-E-A-T & AI Citations
structured_data_author_removed.h1: Author removed from structured data: why it matters for AI
structured_data_author_removed.metaDescription: The author field is gone from your JSON-LD. Attribution is an E-E-A-T signal for Google and a cue for AI systems deciding what to cite. Cause and fix.
structured_data_author_removed.tldr: Removing the author field from your structured data drops an attribution signal that feeds Google's E-E-A-T logic and generative engines' citation decisions. The impact on classic ranking is low, but attributed content starts with one more trust signal: restore the author field in the JSON-LD.
whenNotAProblem: The page is not editorial (product page, category, form): author attribution has no meaning there.
The author is still shown visibly in the content and via another source, even though it left the JSON-LD.
You deliberately swapped a person author for an organization entity that fits your strategy.

exemple.note: A named author, ideally linked to an author page, is more usable than a plain text string.

actionSteps: Open the page's JSON-LD and confirm the author field is truly gone (not just changed in value).
Trace the cause in the template: removed author component, missing data, CMS migration.
Restore a structured author (Person or Organization type) with a name and, if possible, an author page url.
Re-crawl to confirm attribution is back in the structured data.
structured_data_author_removed.scanHook: Has your content gone anonymous in the eyes of AI systems? Enter a URL: Seogard reads the raw HTML and spots the author field disappearing from your JSON-LD. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Is the author field enough to get cited by ChatGPT or Perplexity?
faq.a: No, no single field triggers a citation. But attributed content starts with one more trust signal than anonymous content when an engine decides what to cite.

faq.q: Should the JSON-LD author be a person or an organization?
faq.a: Both are valid. A named person linked to a detailed author page usually carries a richer E-E-A-T signal than a plain organization.

rec_img_alt_audit.constat: Some images on the page have no alt attribute.
rec_img_alt_audit.pourquoi: Google Images cannot understand these images, and neither can screen readers. Modest impact on overall SEO, real impact on image traffic and accessibility.
rec_img_alt_audit.action: Add a descriptive alt to content images (decorative images can keep an empty alt).
rec_img_alt_audit.gain: Google Images traffic becomes possible and the page is accessible.
rec_img_alt_audit.seoTitle: Images Missing Alt Text: Image SEO & Accessibility
rec_img_alt_audit.h1: Images without alt: what Google Images and your visitors lose
rec_img_alt_audit.metaDescription: Some images on your page have no alt attribute. Google Images cannot understand them and neither can screen readers. When to act and how to fix it.
rec_img_alt_audit.tldr: Images with no alt attribute stay unreadable to Google Images and to screen readers, costing your page image traffic and accessibility. The impact on overall SEO is modest: add a descriptive alt to content images and leave an empty alt on decorative ones.
whenNotAProblem: The affected images are purely decorative: an empty alt attribute (alt="") is then the correct practice.
Your images load as CSS backgrounds and are not meant to be indexed as content.
The page has no image-traffic stake (app interface, back office) and stays accessible another way.

exemple.note: The alt describes the image concretely, without keyword stuffing or a prefix like image of.

actionSteps: Find the content images with no alt attribute (products, illustrations, diagrams).
Write a short alt describing the actual content of each image.
Set alt="" on purely decorative images so screen readers skip them.
Re-crawl the page to confirm every content image now has an alt.
rec_img_alt_audit.scanHook: How many of your images are invisible to Google Images? Enter a URL: Seogard analyzes the rendered page and lists the images with no alt attribute. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Must every image have an alt attribute?
faq.a: Yes, but with a descriptive alt for content images and an empty alt (alt="") for decorative ones, so screen readers skip them.

faq.q: Does a missing alt lower my ranking?
faq.a: The impact on overall ranking is modest. The real stakes are Google Images traffic and accessibility for people using a screen reader.

rec_title_length_audit.constat: The title is too short or too long to display fully in Google.
rec_title_length_audit.pourquoi: A truncated title loses its click appeal; a title that is too short underuses the space. This is CTR optimization, not ranking — useful, never urgent.
rec_title_length_audit.action: Aim for a 30-60 character title that carries the main intent.
rec_title_length_audit.gain: A fully visible, more clickable title in search results.
rec_title_length_audit.seoTitle: SEO Title Length: Too Short or Too Long?
rec_title_length_audit.h1: Meta title length: the range that displays in full
rec_title_length_audit.metaDescription: Your meta title is too short or too long, and Google may truncate or rewrite it. The right range and how to adjust it for click-through rate.
rec_title_length_audit.tldr: A title that is too long gets truncated in results and loses its click appeal; too short, it underuses the available space. This is CTR optimization, not ranking: aim for roughly 30 to 60 characters carrying the page's main intent.
whenNotAProblem: The brand at the end of the title spills over slightly: Google often trims it with no real loss of meaning.
The short title is already perfectly explicit (a product page with a short, clear name).
Google rewrites your title for this query anyway: the exact length becomes secondary.

exemple.avant: <title>Home</title>
exemple.apres: <title>Men's trail running shoes: picks and sizing guide</title>
exemple.note: A good title carries the search intent in its first words and fits the width Google displays.

actionSteps: Find the titles under 15 characters or over 60 characters.
Rewrite them so the main intent sits in the first words.
Aim for roughly 30 to 60 characters, brand included if it is short.
Re-crawl to confirm the title now fits the displayed width.
rec_title_length_audit.scanHook: Do your titles display in full in Google? Enter a URL: Seogard reads the title served in the raw HTML and flags the ones likely to be truncated. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: What is the right length for a meta title?
faq.a: Roughly 30 to 60 characters. Beyond that, Google often truncates the title; below it, you underuse valuable space for click appeal.

faq.q: Does a too-long title lower my ranking?
faq.a: No, title length is a CTR concern, not a ranking one. A truncated title stays indexed but may draw fewer clicks in the results.

rec_description_length_audit.constat: The meta description is too short or too long.
rec_description_length_audit.pourquoi: Too long, it gets truncated; too short, Google often replaces it with an excerpt of its own choosing. A pure CTR concern.
rec_description_length_audit.action: Aim for 70 to 155 characters with a clear promise.
rec_description_length_audit.gain: A description sized to display fully when Google chooses to use it — knowing it frequently rewrites snippets based on the query.
rec_description_length_audit.seoTitle: Meta Description Length: The Range That Displays
rec_description_length_audit.h1: Meta description length: sizing the snippet for clicks
rec_description_length_audit.metaDescription: Your meta description is too short or too long. Too long, it gets truncated; too short, Google replaces it. The right range and how to fix it.
rec_description_length_audit.tldr: A meta description that is too long gets truncated in results, and one that is too short often pushes Google to replace it with an excerpt of its own. This is a pure CTR concern: aim for roughly 70 to 155 characters with a clear promise.
whenNotAProblem: You have no meta description: Google then generates an excerpt, which is sometimes better than a weak description.
Google systematically rewrites the snippet for this query, whatever your description says.
The page has no organic traffic stake (technical page, redirect, interface).

exemple.avant: <meta name="description" content="Shoes.">
exemple.apres: <meta name="description" content="Men's trail running shoes: support, grip and a sizing guide to pick the right pair for your terrain.">
exemple.note: The description fits the displayed width and carries a concrete promise that invites the click.

actionSteps: Find the descriptions under 50 characters or over 160 characters.
Rewrite them to around 70 to 155 characters with a clear promise.
Put the most compelling information first, before the possible truncation point.
Re-crawl to confirm the description fits the displayed width.
rec_description_length_audit.scanHook: Do your descriptions display in full in the results? Enter a URL: Seogard reads the meta description served in the raw HTML and flags the ones out of range. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: What is the right length for a meta description?
faq.a: Roughly 70 to 155 characters. Longer, it gets truncated; shorter, Google often replaces it with a page excerpt it deems more relevant.

faq.q: Does Google always use my meta description?
faq.a: No. Google frequently rewrites the snippet based on the query. A well-sized description increases the odds it uses yours as written.

rec_h1_missing_audit.constat: No H1 detected on the page (even after JavaScript rendering).
rec_h1_missing_audit.pourquoi: Without a main title, Google and AI systems have to infer the page's topic from other signals — less reliable. Different from a regression: here the page never had an H1.
rec_h1_missing_audit.action: Add an editorial H1 describing the page's topic.
rec_h1_missing_audit.gain: An explicit topic for every engine, humans included.
rec_h1_missing_audit.seoTitle: Page With No H1: Does It Matter and Should You Add One?
rec_h1_missing_audit.h1: Page with no H1: what Google and AI do without a main title
rec_h1_missing_audit.metaDescription: Your page has no H1, even after JavaScript rendering. Without a main title, Google and AI infer the topic from other signals. Does it matter, and how to fix it.
rec_h1_missing_audit.tldr: Without an H1, Google and AI systems have to guess your page's topic from other signals, which is less reliable. This is not a regression but a structural gap: add a single editorial H1 that clearly describes the page's topic.
whenNotAProblem: The page is an app interface or a login screen with no real content to rank.
A main title exists but with another styled tag: check that a proper semantic H1 carries it.
The page is not meant to be indexed and targets no organic traffic.

exemple.avant: Trail running shoes
exemple.apres: Men's trail running shoes
exemple.note: One editorial H1 per page that reflects the real topic and the main search intent.

actionSteps: Display the rendered page and confirm no H1 tag is present.
Identify the current main title, often styled as a div or an H2.
Turn it into a single semantic H1 describing the page's topic.
Re-crawl to confirm a filled H1 is now present.
rec_h1_missing_audit.scanHook: Do all your pages have a main title? Enter a URL: Seogard analyzes the rendered page and the raw HTML and tells you whether an H1 really exists. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Can a page rank without an H1?
faq.a: Yes, Google can rank a page with no H1 using other signals. But a clear H1 makes the topic explicit and makes engines' and AI's understanding more reliable.

faq.q: How is this different from the missing-H1 regression alert?
faq.a: This page covers a page that never had an H1 (best-practice audit). The regression flags an H1 that existed before a deploy and then disappeared.

rec_h1_missing_in_ssr.constat: The H1 is missing from the raw HTML sent by your server, but present after JavaScript loads.
rec_h1_missing_in_ssr.pourquoi: Google indexes the raw HTML first: your main title is only seen on the second rendering pass, with a delay of 24 hours to several weeks. Generative engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude) read almost exclusively the raw HTML — to them, this page has no title.
rec_h1_missing_in_ssr.action: Render the H1 server-side (SSR), not via a client-hydrated component.
rec_h1_missing_in_ssr.gain: Google captures your title signal on the first crawl, and LLMs have a title to understand and cite the page.
rec_h1_missing_in_ssr.seoTitle: H1 Missing from Raw HTML: The Title AI Never Sees
rec_h1_missing_in_ssr.h1: JS-only H1: why AI systems don't see your main title
rec_h1_missing_in_ssr.metaDescription: Your H1 is missing from the raw HTML but filled in by JavaScript. Google sees it late, LLMs almost never. Render it server-side to be seen from the first crawl.
rec_h1_missing_in_ssr.tldr: Your H1 is empty in the raw HTML the server sends and only appears after JavaScript runs: Google captures it only on its second rendering pass, with a delay, and generative engines almost never see it. Render the H1 server-side (SSR) so it is read on the very first crawl.
whenNotAProblem: The page is intentionally client-rendered and not meant to be indexed or cited.
The H1 is present in the raw HTML and only a secondary subtitle is injected with JavaScript.
You are on the very first crawl of a new page: Seogard does not alert without a baseline.

exemple.apres: Men's trail running shoes
exemple.note: On the left, the H1 depends on JavaScript; on the right, it is already in the raw HTML that Google and AI read first.

actionSteps: View the page's real source (Ctrl+U, not the inspector) and look for the H1 tag.
If the H1 is missing or empty, find the component that renders it client-side only.
Render the H1 server-side (SSR or pre-render) so it sits in the response HTML.
Re-crawl to confirm the H1 is present in the raw HTML.
rec_h1_missing_in_ssr.scanHook: Is your H1 in the raw HTML or only added by JavaScript? Enter a URL: Seogard compares what your server returns with what the browser renders. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does Google eventually see my JavaScript-injected H1?
faq.a: Yes, on its second rendering pass, but with a variable delay: seconds at the median, much longer for crawl-budget-starved pages (raw HTML stays faster to crawl than JavaScript). Generative engines like ChatGPT or Perplexity almost never run it.

faq.a: View the source (Ctrl+U) without running JavaScript: if the H1 is already there and filled, it is server-rendered and readable by every engine on the first crawl.
