Make sure these tags are present in the raw HTML, not just injected by JavaScript (otherwise social crawlers do not see them).
Re-share the link to confirm the preview shows an image and title.
rec_og_missing_audit.scanHook: Do your shared links show an image? Enter a URL: Seogard checks whether your Open Graph tags are in the raw HTML, the version read by LinkedIn, Slack and WhatsApp crawlers. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Do Open Graph tags help with Google ranking?
faq.a: No, they have no effect on Google ranking. They only control the preview shown when your link is shared on social networks and messaging apps.

faq.q: Why does my preview not update after I fix it?
faq.a: Platforms cache the old preview. Use their debug tool (for example LinkedIn's inspector) to force a fresh scrape of the page.

rec_internal_links_audit.constat: The page contains very few outgoing internal links (or none at all).
rec_internal_links_audit.pourquoi: A page that points to nothing is a dead end: it passes no link equity to other pages and does not help Google continue its crawl. This rule covers the page's OUTGOING links (the ones it sends to the rest of the site).
rec_internal_links_audit.action: Add contextual links to topically related pages.
rec_internal_links_audit.gain: The page passes internal link equity and smooths the site's crawl.
rec_internal_links_audit.seoTitle: Too Few Internal Links: The SEO Dead-End Page
rec_internal_links_audit.h1: Too few internal links: when a page points to nothing
rec_internal_links_audit.metaDescription: A page with no outgoing internal links is a dead end: it passes no equity and does not help Google continue its crawl. How to re-link it into your site.
rec_internal_links_audit.tldr: A page with few or no outgoing internal links is a dead end: it passes no equity to other pages and does not help Google continue its crawl. Add contextual links to your topically related pages.
whenNotAProblem: The page is intentionally isolated (paid landing page or conversion funnel where you want zero distraction).
It already has relevant links but inside a client-injected component: in that case check they also exist in the raw HTML.
This is a recommendation (info) as long as at least one link remains; it becomes a warning only if the page has no internal links at all.

exemple.avant:
Trail running guide

Trail running needs good gear...
exemple.apres:
Trail running guide

Trail running needs good trail shoes and a hydration pack.
exemple.note: A good internal link is contextual and descriptive: the anchor describes the target page, not a vague click here.

actionSteps: Spot 2 to 4 concepts on the page that match other pages on your site.
Add a contextual link on each one, with a descriptive anchor (the target page's topic).
Check these links are in the raw HTML, not only generated after JavaScript hydration.
Prioritize links to your strategic pages (categories, pillar pages) to concentrate internal signal.
rec_internal_links_audit.scanHook: Do your deep pages point back into the rest of your site? Enter a URL: Seogard counts your internal links in both the raw HTML and the JavaScript render, and spots dead-end pages. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: How many internal links should a page have?
faq.a: There is no magic number. What matters is that each page points to a few relevant pages rather than being a dead end; Seogard mainly alerts when a page has no outgoing links at all.

faq.q: Does this rule cover incoming or outgoing links?
faq.a: Outgoing links: the ones the page sends to the rest of the site. An orphan page (with no incoming links) is a different issue, not covered by this rule.

rec_llms_txt_missing.pourquoi: Honestly: a speculative convention, not confirmed by major LLMs (neither Google nor OpenAI has adopted it). Its current value is documentary — guiding agents and tools to your key content. Treat it as a bonus, not a priority.
rec_llms_txt_missing.action: If the cost is zero for you: publish an llms.txt listing your reference pages. Otherwise, skip it without regret.
rec_llms_txt_missing.gain: A clean entry point for AI agents — at worst useless, never harmful.
rec_llms_txt_missing.seoTitle: llms.txt: Do You Really Need One in 2026?
rec_llms_txt_missing.h1: Missing llms.txt: is it actually a problem for your AI visibility?
rec_llms_txt_missing.metaDescription: No llms.txt file on your site? Honest truth: no major AI engine confirms using it and Google says it is not necessary. Our take on whether to bother.
rec_llms_txt_missing.tldr: A missing llms.txt is not a problem: it is a speculative convention that no major AI engine confirms using, and Google has said it is not necessary. Publish one only if it costs you nothing; otherwise skip it without regret.
whenNotAProblem: In almost every case: no major AI crawler has confirmed reading llms.txt, so its absence costs you nothing measurable today.
Your content is already well structured and available in the raw HTML: AI engines read it directly, with no need for a dedicated index.
This is an opportunity recommendation (info), never a regression: not having one triggers no penalty or deindexing.

exemple.avant:
# No file at the root
# https://example.com/llms.txt -> 404
exemple.apres:
# https://example.com/llms.txt
# Example Inc - product documentation

## Docs
- [Getting started](https://example.com/docs/start): install guide
- [API](https://example.com/docs/api): full reference
exemple.note: Simple Markdown at the root. Mainly useful for documentation or agents; it replaces neither the sitemap nor robots.txt.

actionSteps: First decide if it is worth it: if publishing a static file costs you more than ten minutes, move on.
If it is trivial, list your reference pages in Markdown (docs, pillar pages) with a short description.
Drop the file at the root, reachable at /llms.txt, without blocking it in robots.txt.
Do not count on any traffic gain: treat it as a clean entry point for agents, useless at worst.
rec_llms_txt_missing.scanHook: Do you really need an llms.txt? Enter a URL: Seogard checks whether it exists and, more importantly, what AI engines actually see in your raw HTML, where your visibility is really decided. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Do major AI models actually use llms.txt?
faq.a: As of today, no major engine (Google, OpenAI, Perplexity) has confirmed reading it. Google has even publicly said it is not necessary. Its value stays documentary.

faq.q: Does not having an llms.txt penalize my site?
faq.a: No, no penalty or deindexing. It is an optional bonus: its absence costs nothing, and its presence guarantees nothing today.

rec_ai_crawlers_blocked.constat: The robots.txt blocks AI citation crawlers (OAI-SearchBot, ChatGPT-User, PerplexityBot, Perplexity-User, Claude-SearchBot, Claude-User).
rec_ai_crawlers_blocked.pourquoi: These crawlers let answer engines (ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Claude) consult and cite your pages: blocking them removes you from their answers. Important nuance: blocking TRAINING crawlers (GPTBot, Google-Extended) does not affect citation — many tools conflate the two, Seogard does not.
rec_ai_crawlers_blocked.action: Allow the citation crawlers in the robots.txt (your policy on training bots can stay unchanged).
rec_ai_crawlers_blocked.gain: Possible presence in AI engine answers — a growing traffic channel.
rec_ai_crawlers_blocked.seoTitle: Should You Block GPTBot and PerplexityBot? The Real Answer
rec_ai_crawlers_blocked.h1: AI citation crawlers blocked: you drop out of ChatGPT and Perplexity answers
rec_ai_crawlers_blocked.metaDescription: Blocking AI citation crawlers (PerplexityBot, ChatGPT-User, OAI-SearchBot) removes you from answers. But blocking training bots does not change citation at all.
rec_ai_crawlers_blocked.tldr: Blocking AI CITATION crawlers (OAI-SearchBot, ChatGPT-User, PerplexityBot, Claude-User) removes you from ChatGPT search, Perplexity and Claude answers. Key nuance: blocking TRAINING crawlers (GPTBot, Google-Extended) does not affect citation, only whether your content is used to train the models.
whenNotAProblem: You deliberately block training crawlers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, Google-Extended) but let citation crawlers through: Seogard does not alert you, it is a legitimate choice.
The block targets a private or paid area you do not want cited anyway (back office, paywalled content).
Your strategy assumes not appearing in AI answer engines: that is a trade-off, not a technical error.

exemple.avant:
# robots.txt
User-agent: PerplexityBot
Disallow: /

User-agent: OAI-SearchBot
Disallow: /
exemple.apres:
# robots.txt
# Citation crawlers allowed
User-agent: PerplexityBot
Allow: /

User-agent: OAI-SearchBot
Allow: /

# Training policy stays your call
User-agent: GPTBot
Disallow: /
exemple.note: You can allow citation while refusing training: these are two distinct bots with two independent policies.

actionSteps: Open your robots.txt and find any Disallow directives targeting OAI-SearchBot, ChatGPT-User, PerplexityBot, Perplexity-User, Claude-SearchBot or Claude-User.
Decide explicitly: do you want to be cited by AI answer engines? If yes, allow these crawlers.
Tell them apart from training bots (GPTBot, Google-Extended): your policy on those can stay at blocked with no impact on citation.
Deploy the fixed robots.txt and confirm it responds at /robots.txt without blocking itself.
rec_ai_crawlers_blocked.scanHook: Are you unintentionally blocking the crawlers that cite you in ChatGPT and Perplexity? Enter a URL: Seogard reads your robots.txt and separates citation from training, where many tools conflate the two. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Should you block GPTBot and PerplexityBot?
faq.a: They serve two different purposes. GPTBot is for model training; PerplexityBot is for citation in answers. Blocking GPTBot does not affect your presence in answers; blocking PerplexityBot removes you from them.

faq.q: What is the difference between a training and a citation crawler?
faq.a: A training crawler collects text to improve a model. A citation crawler reads your page live to answer a user and cite you. Only the second one influences your visibility in AI answers.

rec_structured_data_incomplete.constat: Structured data exists but is incomplete (missing author, date or publisher).
rec_structured_data_incomplete.pourquoi: AI systems rely on these fields to judge reliability and attribute a citation (who wrote it, when, for whom). A skeletal schema yields little.
rec_structured_data_incomplete.action: Fill in author, datePublished and publisher in the JSON-LD.
rec_structured_data_incomplete.gain: Dated, attributed content — the profile generative engines prefer to cite.
rec_structured_data_incomplete.seoTitle: Incomplete Structured Data: Author, Date, Publisher
rec_structured_data_incomplete.h1: Incomplete structured data: when your JSON-LD skips who, when and for whom
rec_structured_data_incomplete.metaDescription: Your JSON-LD exists but has no author, date or publisher? AI engines rely on these fields to judge reliability and attribute a citation. How to complete it.
rec_structured_data_incomplete.tldr: Your structured data exists but skips the author, date or publisher: AI engines rely on exactly these fields to judge reliability and attribute a citation. Fill in author, datePublished and publisher in your JSON-LD.
whenNotAProblem: The page is not editorial content (contact page, cart, category): the notion of author and date has no meaning.
The missing fields are intentionally omitted as irrelevant for this schema type (for example a Product has no author).
This is an improvement recommendation (info): a partial schema stays valid and triggers no error or penalty.

exemple.note: JSON-LD values must match what is visible on the page: an author present in the schema but absent from the text is a contradictory signal.

actionSteps: Open the page's JSON-LD and find the author, datePublished and publisher fields.
Fill in the missing ones with values consistent with the page's visible content.
Check this JSON-LD is present in the raw HTML, not only injected after JavaScript hydration.
Validate the schema with Google's Rich Results Test to confirm it produces no error.
rec_structured_data_incomplete.scanHook: Does your content say who wrote it and when? Enter a URL: Seogard reads your JSON-LD in the raw HTML and flags missing author, date and publisher fields. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Why do author and date matter to AI engines?
faq.a: A generative engine tries to attribute a citation: who wrote it, when, for which organization. Dated, attributed content is easier to cite than anonymous content.

faq.q: Is an incomplete schema an error?
faq.a: No, a partial schema stays technically valid. This is an improvement recommendation: completing it strengthens your citation signals, but its absence triggers no penalty.

rec_faq_schema_missing.constat: A substantial content page (more than 300 words) without a FAQPage schema.
rec_faq_schema_missing.pourquoi: When content addresses recurring questions, organizing it as explicit questions and answers makes it easier for AI answer engines to extract, as they look for passages that answer an intent. It is the visible editorial STRUCTURE (question as a heading, answer below) that carries this benefit, not the FAQPage markup — Google no longer shows FAQ rich results in the SERP. On a page that does not address questions, this recommendation does not apply.
rec_faq_schema_missing.action: If your content answers recurring questions, structure them as clear sections (question as an H2/H3, concise answer below). The FAQPage schema remains optional and harmless, but no longer triggers any enhanced display.
rec_faq_schema_missing.gain: Passages that answer engines can isolate and pick up — and content that is easier for your visitors to scan.
rec_faq_schema_missing.seoTitle: FAQ Schema: Still Useful Since Rich Results Ended?
rec_faq_schema_missing.h1: Missing FAQ schema: myth and reality since 2023
rec_faq_schema_missing.metaDescription: Google stopped showing FAQ rich results in the SERP in 2023. So is the FAQPage schema still worth anything? What actually matters today.
rec_faq_schema_missing.tldr: The FAQPage schema no longer triggers a rich result in the SERP since 2023: what matters now is not the markup but the visible editorial STRUCTURE (question as a heading, answer below) that helps AI answer engines extract your passages. Structure your content as clear Q&A first; the markup stays optional.
whenNotAProblem: The page does not cover recurring questions (product page, category, narrative content): forcing an artificial FAQ would be counterproductive.
Your content is already structured as clear questions and answers in the visible text: the main benefit is already captured, with or without markup.
This is a recommendation (info): the absence of FAQPage triggers no penalty and removes no display, since there is no FAQ rich result anymore.

exemple.avant:
Shipping? Within 48h.

Returns? 30 days.
exemple.apres:
What are the shipping times?

We ship within 48 hours across mainland France.

What is the return policy?

You have 30 days to return an item.
exemple.note: It is the visible structure (question as an H2, concise answer below) that carries the benefit, not the FAQPage markup. The schema stays an optional bonus.

actionSteps: First check the page genuinely covers recurring questions; if not, this recommendation does not apply.
Structure each question as an H2 or H3, with a concise, self-contained answer right below.
Put a short, direct answer at the start of each section: that is what answer engines isolate.
Optional: add the FAQPage schema as JSON-LD; harmless, but do not expect any enhanced SERP display.
rec_faq_schema_missing.scanHook: Is your FAQ content actually extractable by AI engines? Enter a URL: Seogard checks the structure of your questions in the raw HTML, the version read by Google and answer engines. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Is the FAQPage schema still worth anything in 2026?
faq.a: It no longer triggers a rich result in the SERP since 2023. It remains clean, harmless markup, but what really helps AI answer engines is the visible editorial structure of your questions and answers.

faq.q: Should I add a FAQ to all my pages?
faq.a: No. A FAQ only makes sense if the page genuinely covers recurring questions. Forcing an artificial FAQ to tick a box dilutes the content with no gain.

rec_citation_signals_missing.constat: Several citation signals are missing (author, date, external sources).
rec_citation_signals_missing.pourquoi: To cite content, a generative engine looks for who is speaking, when, and on what basis. Anonymous, undated content with no references starts at a disadvantage against an attributed competitor.
rec_citation_signals_missing.action: Display the author and date, and back key claims with links to sources.
rec_citation_signals_missing.gain: Verifiable content (who is speaking, when, on what basis) — the elements a generative engine can use to evaluate a source. Not a citation guarantee, but one less disadvantage.
rec_citation_signals_missing.seoTitle: Weak Citation Signals: Why AI Engines Skip You
rec_citation_signals_missing.h1: Weak citation signals: anonymous, undated content with no sources
rec_citation_signals_missing.metaDescription: Content with no author, no date and no sources starts at a disadvantage with generative engines. How to make it verifiable and citable.
rec_citation_signals_missing.tldr: Anonymous, undated content with no sources starts at a disadvantage: to cite you, a generative engine looks for who is speaking, when, and on what basis. Display the author and date, and back your key claims with links to sources.
whenNotAProblem: The page is not informational content (transactional page, form, category): author, date and sources have no place there.
Your content already shows author, date and references, but inside client-injected elements: check they also exist in the raw HTML.
This is an improvement recommendation (info): these signals guarantee no citation, but their absence is an avoidable disadvantage.

exemple.avant:
Trail running is growing in France

The sport has exploded in recent years.
exemple.apres:
Trail running is growing in France

By Marie Dupont, July 9, 2026

The sport is growing, according to this federation study.
exemple.note: Backing a key claim with a link to a reliable reference makes the content verifiable, which a generative engine values when choosing a source.

actionSteps: Clearly display the content's author (real name, ideally with an author page) and the publication or update date.
Identify your key factual claims and add a link to a reliable external source for each one.
Mirror these same signals in the JSON-LD (author, datePublished) so they are machine-readable.
Check that author, date and links are present in the raw HTML, not only in the JavaScript render.
rec_citation_signals_missing.scanHook: Does your content say who is speaking, when, and on what basis? Enter a URL: Seogard checks your citation signals (author, date, sources) in the raw HTML that generative engines read. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Do these signals guarantee a citation by ChatGPT or Perplexity?
faq.a: No guarantee. But anonymous, undated content starts at a disadvantage against an attributed, sourced competitor: these signals remove an obstacle, they do not force a citation.

faq.q: How is this different from incomplete structured data?
faq.a: Structured data covers the machine fields of the JSON-LD. Citation signals cover the whole picture, including human-visible elements: displayed author, readable date and links to sources.

rec_content_structure_audit.constat: The content has neither subheadings (H2) nor lists.
rec_content_structure_audit.pourquoi: AI systems extract passages: an unstructured wall of text is hard to break up, so cited less. Humans also scan structured content better — a double win.
rec_content_structure_audit.action: Break the content into H2 sections and convert enumerations into lists.
rec_content_structure_audit.gain: Content extractable by AI systems and more readable for your visitors.
rec_content_structure_audit.seoTitle: Unstructured Content: The Wall of Text AI Skips
rec_content_structure_audit.h1: Content structure: why a wall of text with no H2 or lists gets cited less
rec_content_structure_audit.metaDescription: Content with no subheadings or lists is a wall of text that is hard to break up: AI engines extract passages, and a wall resists them. How to structure it.
rec_content_structure_audit.tldr: Content with no subheadings (H2) or lists is a wall of text that AI engines struggle to break up, so they cite it less, because they extract precise passages. Structure the text into H2 sections and turn your enumerations into lists: a double win for AI and for your readers.
whenNotAProblem: The content is already well structured into sections and lists, but inside client-injected elements: check it is also in the raw HTML.
The page is very short or narrative by nature (an editorial, a news brief) where splitting into H2 would feel artificial.
This is a recommendation (info): lightly structured text still gets indexed, it is just less easy for answer engines to extract.

exemple.avant:
Choosing trail running shoes

You need to look at the drop, the grip, the cushioning, the waterproofing and the weight, each criterion mattering depending on the terrain...
exemple.apres:
Choosing trail running shoes

The criteria that matter

  • Drop: heel-to-toe height.
  • Grip: lug depth.
  • Cushioning: comfort over long distances.
exemple.note: One H2 per idea and one list per enumeration give answer engines clean blocks to isolate, and your readers scannable content.

actionSteps: Spot the main ideas in the content and give each one an explicit H2 subheading.
Turn enumerations buried in a paragraph into real bulleted or numbered lists.
Put a summary sentence at the start of each section: that is the passage an answer engine isolates.
Check this structure (H2 and lists) is present in the raw HTML, not only after JavaScript hydration.
rec_content_structure_audit.scanHook: Is your content a wall of text or a structure AI can extract? Enter a URL: Seogard analyzes your subheadings and lists in the raw HTML, the version Google and answer engines break up. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Why does structure help AI engines cite content?
faq.a: Generative engines extract precise passages to answer a question. Content split into sections and lists offers clean blocks to isolate; a uniform wall of text is harder to break up, so picked up less.

faq.q: Should I add H2s everywhere, even on short text?
faq.a: No. On very short or narrative content, forcing subheadings would feel artificial. Structure should serve readability; this recommendation targets longer content that would benefit from being broken up.

perf_page_weight_explosion.constat: The page's total weight increased sharply since the previous crawl.
perf_page_weight_explosion.pourquoi: A suddenly heavier page loads more slowly, especially on mobile — degraded experience and Core Web Vitals under pressure. The typical cause: an uncompressed image or a JS dependency added unchecked.
perf_page_weight_explosion.action: Identify the responsible resource (image, script) in the weight breakdown and compress or remove it.
perf_page_weight_explosion.gain: Load time under control — page weight is one of the direct levers on LCP, especially on mobile.
perf_page_weight_explosion.seoTitle: Page Weight Explosion: Causes and How to Fix It
perf_page_weight_explosion.h1: Page weight explosion: when a page suddenly gets too heavy
perf_page_weight_explosion.metaDescription: A page's total weight jumped from one crawl to the next, slowing load times, especially on mobile. Where the increase comes from, how to spot and fix it.
perf_page_weight_explosion.tldr: A page weight explosion means the total bytes downloaded jumped from one crawl to the next, which slows loading, especially on mobile. In the weight breakdown, find the added resource (uncompressed image, script) and trim it down.
whenNotAProblem: You deliberately enriched the page (new video, product gallery) and the weight stays reasonable for the content added.
The increase comes from a one-off third-party resource (campaign banner) that will drop off on its own.
The page is not meant to be indexed or visited at scale (internal page, admin tool): speed matters less there.

exemple.note: A single unoptimized image is often enough to blow up the weight; converting it to WebP/AVIF brings it back to its proper size.

actionSteps: Open the page weight breakdown (HTML, JS, CSS, images, fonts) to see which category ballooned.
Compare with the previous crawl to isolate the resource or resources added between the two versions.
Compress the offending resource: image to WebP/AVIF, minified or deferred script, dependency removed if useless.
Redeploy, then re-crawl the page to confirm the weight has dropped back to a normal level.
perf_page_weight_explosion.scanHook: Did one of your pages get too heavy after a deploy? Enter a URL: Seogard measures the real page weight and its breakdown, and tells you what ballooned. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: At what weight is a page too heavy?
faq.a: Lighthouse sets the warning threshold around 1.6 MB and excess beyond 5 MB; but this alert is about the sharp increase versus the previous crawl, not an absolute value.

faq.q: Does page weight really affect SEO?
faq.a: Indirectly: high weight slows loading, which degrades the experience and the Core Web Vitals that Google measures. There is no direct penalty tied to the byte count alone.

rec_perf_page_heavy.constat: The page exceeds 1.6 MB (Lighthouse threshold), excessive beyond 5 MB.
rec_perf_page_heavy.pourquoi: Weight hits your mobile visitors and bounce rate first; the SEO effect works through experience (Core Web Vitals), not a direct penalty. Treat it as ongoing groundwork, not an emergency.
rec_perf_page_heavy.action: Compress images (modern formats) and trim the embedded JavaScript.
rec_perf_page_heavy.gain: Faster pages for real visitors — the metric that comes before all the others.
rec_perf_page_heavy.seoTitle: Heavy Page: Why It Matters and How to Trim It
rec_perf_page_heavy.h1: Heavy page: when weight slows your visitors down
rec_perf_page_heavy.metaDescription: Your page is over the recommended weight threshold, so it loads slower for mobile visitors. What it changes, when to care and how to trim it down.
rec_perf_page_heavy.tldr: A heavy page is over the Lighthouse weight threshold (around 1.6 MB, excessive beyond 5 MB) and loads slowly, especially on mobile. This is ongoing groundwork, not an emergency: compress images and trim the embedded JavaScript.
whenNotAProblem: The page serves an inherently large media (video, heavy visualization) and the weight is justified by its function.
Your visitors are mostly on fast connections and desktop, where weight is felt far less.
It is a rarely visited or non-indexed page: other, more strategic pages deserve the effort first.

exemple.note: Images are almost always the biggest item; modern formats and lazy-loading are often enough to get under the threshold.

actionSteps: Look at the weight breakdown to find the dominant item (most often images, sometimes JavaScript).
Convert images to modern formats (WebP/AVIF), size them to their display dimensions and enable lazy-loading.
Trim the JavaScript: remove useless dependencies, minify and defer anything not critical to the first render.
Re-crawl the page to confirm the total weight is back under the recommended threshold.
rec_perf_page_heavy.scanHook: Are your pages too heavy for your mobile visitors? Enter a URL: Seogard measures the real page weight and details its breakdown, image by image. Free, no credit card.

faq.q: Does Google penalize a heavy page?
faq.a: Not directly: Google does not penalize high weight as such. The effect works through speed and Core Web Vitals, which shape the experience and, at the margin, ranking.

faq.q: What is the biggest lever to lighten a page?
faq.a: Almost always images: compressing them to WebP/AVIF, sizing them to their display size and lazy-loading them cuts weight faster than any other change.
